Confusing the Network tab with other dev tools like the Console or Elements tab.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Explains the purpose of the Network tab (to monitor network requests) (1 mark). • Explains how it is used to diagnose issues, such as identifying large files or long-running requests, with reference to the waterfall chart (2 marks). • Explains how the diagnosis leads to specific optimisations, such as compressing images or minifying CSS/JS (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: A developer uses the Network tab to diagnose a slow-loading page by analysing all the network requests the browser makes to render the page. The tab displays a list of all assets (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, images) and API calls. The 'waterfall' chart is a key diagnostic tool, as it visually shows how long each asset takes to download. A developer would first look for assets with a large file size, such as uncompressed images or large JavaScript libraries, which are common causes of slow loads. They would also look for requests that have a long 'Time to First Byte' (TTFB), which can indicate a slow back-end or API response. Once the problematic assets are identified, the developer can take specific optimisation actions. For example, if a large image is the problem, they can compress it or serve it in a more modern format like WebP. If a CSS or JavaScript file is large, they can 'minify' it to reduce its size. This cause-and-effect process of using the Network tab to diagnose bottlenecks and then applying targeted optimisations is a fundamental workflow for web performance tuning.

The Network tab shows all the files a webpage has to download. If a page is slow, a developer can look in this tab to see if any files are really big, like a huge image. They can also see how long each file takes to load. If they find a big image, they can make it smaller to optimise the page and make it load faster.
The Network tab shows the internet. If it is slow you can fix it.

prompts.question: Outline two ways a web browser's developer tools can be used to debug the front-end of a web application.
prompts.scenario: A JavaScript function is not working as expected. A developer opens the browser's developer tools to begin the debugging process.
markerNotes: Look for two distinct uses.
Acceptable answers include: inspecting the DOM (Elements tab), viewing errors (Console), logging variables (Console), setting breakpoints (Sources), inspecting network traffic (Network).
The answer only needs to state the main features, not provide deep detail.
commonStudentErrors: Being too vague (e.g., 'it finds bugs').
Naming the same feature twice.
Confusing front-end debugging with back-end debugging.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Outlines one valid use of dev tools for debugging (1 mark). • Outlines a second, distinct valid use (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: Firstly, the 'Console' tab can be used to view error messages and to log the values of variables at different points in the code using console.log(). Secondly, the 'Sources' tab can be used to set breakpoints, which pause the execution of JavaScript code at a specific line, allowing the developer to inspect the state of the application and step through the code line by line.
You can use the console to see errors and you can also use it to see what's in a variable.
It helps you find bugs.

prompts.question: Explain how Lighthouse audits can be used to evaluate and improve the performance, accessibility, and SEO of a web application.
prompts.scenario: A web agency is preparing to launch a client’s new site. Before going live, they run Lighthouse and receive low scores in several categories.
markerNotes: Expect mention of Performance, Accessibility, Best Practices, SEO scores and specific metrics (FCP, TBT, CLS, etc.).
commonStudentErrors: Thinking Lighthouse only measures speed.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Describes what Lighthouse measures (2 marks) • Explains how diagnostics lead to concrete improvements (3 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: Lighthouse is an open-source automated auditing tool integrated into Chrome DevTools that evaluates a page against Performance, Accessibility, Best Practices, SEO, and PWA criteria. It provides scored reports and actionable diagnostics. For performance it measures Core Web Vitals (LCP, FID/TBT, CLS) and highlights issues like render-blocking resources or unminified assets. For accessibility it checks contrast ratios, ARIA labels, and semantic HTML. For SEO it verifies meta tags, hreflang, and mobile-friendliness. By following Lighthouse’s specific recommendations (e.g., defer offscreen images, use alt attributes, add meta description), developers can systematically improve the site across multiple dimensions before launch.

dotPoints.description: Explore the types and significance of code libraries for front-end web development

prompts.question: Evaluate the use of a large, all-in-one front-end framework (e.g., Angular) versus a more flexible, library-based approach (e.g., using React with a collection of smaller, independent libraries) for a long-term, enterprise-scale project.
prompts.scenario: An architectural review board at a large enterprise is debating the standard front-end technology for all new projects. One group advocates for a single, all-in-one framework to ensure consistency, while another argues for a more flexible, library-based approach to allow for innovation. You are asked to write an evaluation of the two approaches.
markerNotes: A top-tier evaluation must be based on clear criteria and an understanding of the trade-offs.
Look for the key distinction: 'opinionated' frameworks vs. flexible, composable libraries.
The answer should discuss the implications of this choice on long-term maintainability, team structure, and adaptability.
commonStudentErrors: Stating a personal preference without a technical evaluation.
Failing to understand the core difference between a framework and a library.
Not applying the evaluation to the specific context of a 'long-term, enterprise-scale' project.
Not making a final, nuanced judgement that acknowledges the trade-offs.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Makes a clear evaluative judgement, acknowledging that both are valid but have different trade-offs (1 mark). • Applies multiple criteria, such as developer onboarding, long-term maintenance, and flexibility (3 marks). • Provides comprehensive evidence for each criterion, contrasting the 'opinionated' nature of a framework with the flexibility of a library-based approach (3 marks). • Synthesises the evaluation into a nuanced conclusion about the trade-offs (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
The choice between a large framework like Angular and a library-based approach like React represents a fundamental architectural trade-off. For a long-term, enterprise-scale project, my evaluation is that while a framework offers initial consistency, a library-based approach often provides superior long-term flexibility, though with higher initial complexity.

A large framework like Angular is 'opinionated'; it provides a comprehensive, all-in-one solution for state management, routing, and data fetching, and it dictates how the application should be structured. The effectiveness of this approach is in consistency and developer onboarding. A new developer joining the team will find a familiar structure and a clear set of established patterns, making the codebase easier to navigate. This can be highly valuable for long-term maintainability in a large team.

A library-based approach with React is more flexible. React itself is just a UI library, requiring the team to choose and integrate other independent libraries for routing, state management, etc. This requires more architectural decisions upfront but provides the flexibility to swap out a single part of the stack (e.g., the state management library) as better technologies emerge, without needing to rewrite the entire application. This adaptability can be crucial over the long lifespan of an enterprise project.

In conclusion, the evaluation depends on the enterprise's priorities. If the goal is maximum consistency and faster onboarding for a large, rotating team, the opinionated framework is highly effective. If the goal is maximum long-term flexibility and the ability to adopt best-in-class tools as they evolve, the library-based approach is superior, provided the team has the architectural expertise to manage it.

For a big project, a framework like Angular is good because it includes everything you need. This makes it easier for new programmers to learn because all the projects look the same. A library like React is just for the UI, so you have to pick other libraries for other things. This is more flexible because you can change parts later, but it can be more confusing. I think the framework is a better evaluation for a big company because it is more organised.
React is better than Angular because more people use it.

prompts.question: Distinguish between a front-end 'framework' and a 'library'.
prompts.scenario: A junior developer refers to React as a 'framework'. A senior developer takes the opportunity to distinguish between the two terms.
markerNotes: The core of a high-scoring answer is the explanation of 'Inversion of Control'.
Look for the distinction: 'you call the library' versus 'the framework calls you'.
Credit answers that use examples like React (library) vs. Angular (framework) to illustrate the point.
commonStudentErrors: Stating that frameworks are just 'bigger' libraries.
Failing to explain the concept of Inversion of Control.
Providing examples without explaining the difference.
Using the terms interchangeably.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Indicates the key difference based on 'Inversion of Control' (the framework calls your code, while you call the library's code) (3 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: The key distinction between a framework and a library is the concept of 'Inversion of Control'. A library is a collection of reusable code that you, the developer, call when you need it. For example, you might call a function from a library to format a date or make an API call. You are in control of the application flow. In contrast, a framework is a scaffold or skeleton of an application that calls your code. The framework dictates the overall structure and lifecycle of the application, and you fill in the specific logic. The framework is in control, and it tells your code when to run, not the other way around.
A library is a set of tools you can use. A framework is a whole system that tells you how to build your app. A framework is bigger and has more rules than a library.
They are both code that someone else wrote.

dotPoints.description: Investigate methods to support and manage the load times of web pages/applications

prompts.question: Analyse how the use of a Content Delivery Network (CDN) can help manage and improve the load times of a global web application.
prompts.scenario: An Australian e-commerce company is expanding into the European market. They notice that European users are complaining that the website is very slow to load. A network engineer is tasked with analysing how a CDN could solve this problem.
markerNotes: A strong analysis must explain both key mechanisms: reducing latency through geographic proximity and reducing origin load through caching.
Look for an understanding of the distributed nature of a CDN.
Credit answers that use correct terminology to describe the process.
commonStudentErrors: Describing a CDN but not analysing how it improves performance.
Only focusing on the latency aspect and ignoring the benefit of reducing origin server load.
Confusing a CDN with a web host or a cloud provider.
Not using key terms like 'caching', 'latency', or 'edge server'.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Identifies the key component: a CDN as a distributed network of servers (1 mark). • Examines the relationship between the CDN and latency, explaining how it serves content from a location geographically closer to the user (2 marks). • Examines the relationship between the CDN and server load, explaining how caching static assets reduces requests to the origin server (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer:
A Content Delivery Network (CDN) is a critical tool for managing and improving the load times of a global web application by addressing two key performance bottlenecks: network latency and origin server load. A CDN is a geographically distributed network of proxy servers that cache a website's static content (like images, CSS, and JavaScript files).

An analysis of its first role shows it directly reduces latency. When a user in Australia requests a webpage, instead of fetching assets from a primary server in the United States, the CDN serves the cached assets from a local Australian 'edge' server. Since the physical distance the data has to travel is much shorter, the round-trip time is significantly reduced, leading to faster load times for the user.

Secondly, the CDN reduces the load on the application's origin server. Because the majority of requests for static assets are handled by the CDN's cache, the origin server is freed up to focus on its primary task: generating dynamic content and handling API requests. This prevents the origin server from becoming a bottleneck during periods of high traffic and improves its overall response time. The conclusion is that a CDN improves performance through a two-pronged approach: it moves content closer to the user to reduce latency and it offloads traffic from the main server to reduce its load.

A CDN is a bunch of servers around the world. It copies your website's images and files onto all of them. When a user visits your site, they get the files from the server closest to them. This makes the website load faster because the data doesn't have to travel as far. It also means your main server doesn't have to do as much work.
A CDN is a network that makes websites fast.

prompts.question: Explain the three Core Web Vitals metrics and how developers can optimise a web application to achieve good scores.
prompts.scenario: Google Search Console is showing poor Core Web Vitals for a news website, affecting its SEO ranking. The development team needs to understand what to fix.
markerNotes: LCP, FID/INP, CLS.
commonStudentErrors: Confusing LCP with page load time.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Correctly defines each metric (3 marks) • Gives practical optimisation techniques for each (3 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) measures loading performance – aim <2.5s by optimising server response, render-blocking resources, and critical CSS. Interaction to Next Paint (INP) measures responsiveness – aim <200ms by reducing main-thread work, debouncing events, and using Web Workers. Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) measures visual stability – aim <0.1 by reserving space for ads/images, using aspect ratios, and avoiding DOM insertions above existing content. Achieving good scores requires a combination of server-side rendering, code splitting, image optimisation, and careful third-party script management.

dotPoints.description: Investigate cascading style sheets (CSS) and its impact on the design of a web application

prompts.question: Evaluate the use of a CSS preprocessor, such as SASS/SCSS, compared to using plain CSS for managing the styling of a large, complex web application.
prompts.scenario: A front-end development team is starting a new enterprise project. Some developers want to use plain CSS for simplicity, while others argue for using a preprocessor like SASS to improve long-term maintainability. The lead developer must evaluate the trade-offs.
markerNotes: The evaluation must be balanced, acknowledging pros and cons for both approaches.
Look for specific features of preprocessors (variables, mixins, nesting) and how they impact maintainability.
The judgement should be contextualised to a 'large, complex' application.
Mentioning the need for a compilation step for preprocessors is a key point of comparison.
commonStudentErrors: Only describing what a preprocessor is without evaluating its use.
Failing to name specific features like variables or mixins.
Not considering the downsides of using a preprocessor (e.g., added complexity, compilation step).
Not linking the features to benefits like consistency and maintainability.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Makes a clear judgement on the value of a preprocessor for a large project (1 mark). • Explains at least two features of a preprocessor (e.g., variables, nesting) (2 marks). • Links these features to specific benefits like consistency and code reusability (2 marks). • Acknowledges a trade-off, such as the need for a build/compile step (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
For a large, complex web application, the use of a CSS preprocessor like SASS is highly effective and justifiable, despite the minor overhead it introduces. My evaluation is that its benefits for maintainability and consistency far outweigh the costs.

Plain CSS can become difficult to manage at scale. It lacks features for code reuse and consistency, often leading to repetition and 'magic numbers' for colours and sizes. A preprocessor like SASS addresses this by introducing programming-like features. Variables allow developers to define a central palette of colours or font sizes, ensuring consistency across hundreds of files. Changing a brand colour becomes a one-line edit instead of a project-wide search-and-replace. Mixins allow for the creation of reusable blocks of styles, which is perfect for common patterns like flexbox centering or button styles, reducing code duplication.

The primary trade-off is the introduction of a compilation step; browsers cannot read SASS directly, so it must be compiled into standard CSS. However, this is a standard, automated part of modern web development workflows and is a small price to pay for the enormous improvement in organisation and long-term maintainability on a large project.

A CSS preprocessor like SASS is better for big projects. It lets you use variables, so you can set a colour once and reuse it everywhere. This makes it consistent. It also lets you nest your styles which makes the code easier to read. Plain CSS doesn't have this, so you have to repeat code a lot. The downside is you need a tool to turn the SASS code into regular CSS that the browser can read.
SASS is a special type of CSS that helps you write code better.

dotPoints.description: Explain the use and development of open-source software in relation to web development

prompts.question: Justify the decision for a startup to build their web application using a stack of open-source technologies (e.g., Linux, Apache, MySQL, PHP/Python - LAMP/LEMP) rather than proprietary alternatives.
prompts.scenario: A startup founder with a limited budget needs to decide on the technology stack for their new e-commerce platform. A consultant recommends using an entirely open-source stack and must justify this recommendation, considering factors beyond just the initial cost.
markerNotes: The justification should be multifaceted, covering cost, community support, and flexibility.
Look for arguments supported by specific characteristics of open-source software.
A strong answer will also acknowledge a potential downside, such as the need for in-house expertise.
Mentioning the large communities and ecosystems around popular open-source projects is key.
commonStudentErrors: Only mentioning that open-source is 'free'.
Failing to consider other factors like security, support, or flexibility.
Not providing a balanced argument that might include a potential drawback.
Confusing open-source with freeware.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Provides a justification based on cost, mentioning zero licensing fees (1 mark). • Provides a justification based on flexibility and avoidance of vendor lock-in (2 marks). • Provides a justification based on the availability of strong community support and a large talent pool (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: Using an open-source stack like LAMP is a strategically sound and justifiable decision for a startup. The most immediate justification is the reduction in cost. There are no licensing fees for the operating system, web server, database, or programming language, which significantly lowers the initial capital expenditure. However, the justification extends beyond cost. Secondly, open-source software provides immense flexibility and avoids vendor lock-in. The startup has the freedom to modify and extend the software to fit their specific needs and can host the application with any provider, unlike proprietary systems that may impose restrictions. Finally, popular open-source projects are supported by massive global communities. This means access to extensive documentation, public forums, and a vast talent pool of developers, making it easier and often cheaper to find support and hire staff compared to specialised, proprietary technologies. While it requires the business to take responsibility for its own support, the combination of cost-effectiveness, flexibility, and community access makes it an ideal choice for a new venture.

A startup should use open-source because it is free, so they save money on licenses. It is also justified because you are not locked into one company's product. You can change it if you need to. Another good reason is that many people use open-source, so it is easy to find help online on forums if you have a problem, and it's easier to hire developers who know the technology.
Startups should use open source because it is free to download.

dotPoints.description: Investigate and explain the role of the W3C including internationalisation

prompts.question: Describe two technical challenges that the W3C Internationalisation (i18n) activity helps web developers address when building a global website.
prompts.scenario: A startup is expanding its web application from Australia to the Middle East and Japan. The developers are facing issues with text layout and character display.
markerNotes: Look for specific examples: Bi-directional text (RTL), Character encodings (UTF-8), Cultural formatting (Dates/Currencies).
prompts.markingCriteria: • Describes the challenge of different character sets and the role of Unicode/UTF-8 (2 marks) • Describes the challenge of text direction (Left-to-Right vs Right-to-Left) and how W3C standards handle this (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer:
One technical challenge is **character encoding**. Different languages use different scripts (e.g., Kanji for Japanese). W3C standards promote the use of Unicode (UTF-8) to ensure all characters are displayed correctly across all browsers, preventing 'mojibake' (garbled text). 

A second challenge is **text direction**. Languages like Arabic and Hebrew are read Right-to-Left (RTL). W3C guidelines provide HTML attributes (like `dir="rtl"`) and CSS properties (like Flexbox/Grid logical properties) to automatically mirror layouts and ensure text flows correctly for these users.

dotPoints.description: Investigate cascading style sheets (CSS) regarding flexibility with browsers or display devices

prompts.question: Explain how CSS Media Queries are used to achieve a responsive web design that works across different display devices.
prompts.scenario: A client complains that their website looks great on a laptop but is unusable on a smartphone because the text is too small and the layout requires horizontal scrolling.
markerNotes: Must explain checking device characteristics (width/resolution).
Must explain conditional application of styles.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Explains that media queries detect device properties like width (1 mark) • Explains that they apply different CSS blocks based on these conditions (breakpoints) (1 mark) • Links this to adapting layout (e.g. multi-column to single-column) for usability (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: CSS Media Queries allow the webpage to detect the specific characteristics of the user's device, most commonly the viewport width. By setting 'breakpoints' (e.g., `@media (max-width: 600px)`), developers can define specific CSS rules that only apply when the screen is smaller than that width. This enables the layout to adapt fluidly—for example, changing a three-column desktop layout into a single-column vertical stack for mobile devices—ensuring content is readable and usable without horizontal scrolling on any screen size.

dotPoints.description: Explore and explain the influence of a web browser on web development (client-side storage)

prompts.question: Compare 'Cookies' and 'LocalStorage' as methods for client-side data storage. Recommend which one should be used to store a user's 'Dark Mode' preference setting.
prompts.scenario: A junior developer is unsure where to save user preferences. They are considering using Cookies but have heard LocalStorage might be better.
markerNotes: Cookies: Sent with every HTTP request (bandwidth overhead), small capacity (4KB), used for Auth.
LocalStorage: Stored strictly on client, larger capacity (5MB), not sent to server.
Recommendation: LocalStorage for UI prefs.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Describes Cookie characteristics (sent to server, small size) (2 marks) • Describes LocalStorage characteristics (client-only, larger size) (2 marks) • Recommends LocalStorage for UI preference because it doesn't need to be sent to the server on every request (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
Cookies are small text files (approx 4KB) that are automatically sent to the server with every HTTP request. They are primarily designed for server-side reading, such as session authentication tokens. LocalStorage provides a larger storage capacity (approx 5MB) and the data resides purely in the browser; it is never transferred to the server unless explicitly sent via JavaScript.

For a 'Dark Mode' preference, **LocalStorage** is the recommended choice. This is a purely cosmetic UI setting that the browser needs to know to render the page, but the server does not necessarily need on every request. Storing it in a Cookie would add unnecessary data overhead to every network call (wasting bandwidth), whereas LocalStorage keeps the data efficient and accessible where it is needed.

dotPoints.description: Explore the types and significance of code libraries including template engines

prompts.question: Describe the function of a server-side template engine (e.g., Jinja2, EJS, Blade) in a web development system.
prompts.scenario: A student is building a dynamic website using Python/Flask and needs to display a list of database items in an HTML table.
markerNotes: Must explain mixing static HTML with dynamic data.
Key concept: Placeholders/Variables replaced by data at runtime.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Identifies purpose: separating logic from presentation (1 mark) • Describes mechanism: combining a static HTML template with dynamic data from the server (1 mark) • Mentions rendering the final HTML sent to the client (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: A server-side template engine functions to combine static HTML structure with dynamic data from the back-end. It allows developers to write HTML files containing special placeholders or logic (like loops and variables). When a request is received, the server processes the template, replacing the placeholders with actual data from the database (e.g., a user's name or a list of products), and 'renders' a final, standard HTML document that is sent to the client browser.

dotPoints.description: Assess the contribution of back-end web development to the success of a web application

prompts.question: Assess the contribution of back-end web development to the success of a modern e-commerce web application.
prompts.scenario: During a project retrospective for a new e-commerce website, the management team wants to understand the value and complexity of the back-end development work compared to the more visible front-end design.
markerNotes: The response must make a judgement (e.g., 'it is critical', 'it is the foundation').
The assessment should be supported by multiple, distinct points that are clearly linked to back-end functions.
Look for examples directly relevant to e-commerce success, such as secure transactions, stock control, and user account management.
Higher marks for discussing concepts like performance and scalability.
commonStudentErrors: Providing a description of the back-end rather than assessing its contribution.
Failing to make a clear judgement on its importance.
Not providing specific examples relevant to an e-commerce context (e.g., payment processing, inventory management).
Focusing on only one aspect, such as the database, while ignoring security or business logic.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Makes a clear judgement on the value and importance of back-end development (1 mark). • Provides detailed points for the judgement, linking back-end functions like data management, security, and business logic to the success of an e-commerce site (4 marks). • Uses appropriate terminology and presents a well-structured argument (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
The contribution of back-end web development is paramount to the success of a modern e-commerce application; it is the engine that drives all critical functionality, security, and performance. While the front-end provides the user interface, the back-end is responsible for the core operations that define the business.

Firstly, the back-end manages all business logic and data processing. This includes inventory management, processing payments, calculating shipping costs, and managing user accounts. Without a robust back-end, product information would be static, and transactions could not be securely processed, rendering the e-commerce site useless. The success of the application is directly tied to the reliability and accuracy of these server-side processes.

Secondly, the back-end is the foundation of the application's security. It handles user authentication and authorisation, protects sensitive customer data (like payment details and addresses) in the database, and secures communication via APIs. A security breach originating from a poorly implemented back-end can lead to catastrophic financial loss, legal penalties, and irreparable damage to customer trust, directly causing the failure of the business.

Finally, the back-end dictates the performance and scalability of the application. An efficient back-end with an optimised database and well-designed APIs ensures that pages load quickly and can handle high traffic volumes, such as during a sale event. A slow or unreliable back-end leads to a poor user experience, cart abandonment, and lost sales.

In assessment, the back-end's contribution is not just significant, it is foundational. While a good front-end is necessary for user engagement, it is the back-end that provides the security, reliability, and functionality that makes a successful e-commerce business possible.
The back-end is important for a website. It does the coding on the server. If the back-end is bad the website won't work and the business will fail.

The back-end is very important for an e-commerce website to succeed. It is responsible for handling the database, which stores all the product information and customer details. The back-end also processes payments when a customer buys something, which is a key part of the site. It also handles user logins to make sure they are secure. So, if the back-end doesn't work well, people can't buy things and their data might not be safe, which means the site would not be successful.

dotPoints.description: Compare Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) to SQL

prompts.question: Compare writing database queries using raw SQL with using an Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) tool.
prompts.scenario: A development team is deciding on the technical stack for a new project. The lead developer needs to explain the trade-offs between using an ORM for rapid development versus writing raw SQL for maximum performance.
markerNotes: Students should demonstrate an understanding of both concepts.
The comparison must highlight both similarities (they both interact with a database) and differences.
Key points of comparison should include developer productivity, performance/optimisation, and the level of abstraction.
Look for examples of what a developer might write in each case (e.g., an SQL string vs. an ORM method call).
commonStudentErrors: Only describing one of the two approaches.
Failing to provide clear points of comparison (e.g., only providing separate definitions).
Making absolute statements like 'ORM is always slower' without nuance.
Not understanding that an ORM generates SQL.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Clearly explains the nature of raw SQL queries (1 mark). • Clearly explains the function of an ORM as an abstraction layer (1 mark). • Provides at least one point of comparison regarding developer experience or productivity (1 mark). • Provides at least one point of comparison regarding performance or control (1 mark). • Uses appropriate terminology and structure (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
Raw SQL and Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) are two different approaches for an application to interact with a relational database, each with distinct advantages and disadvantages.

Writing raw SQL involves a developer manually writing SQL statements (e.g., SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE) as strings within the application code. This approach provides maximum control and flexibility, allowing for highly optimised and complex queries that can leverage database-specific features. The main drawback is that it can be tedious, error-prone (e.g., SQL injection vulnerabilities if not handled carefully), and it tightly couples the application to a specific database schema and vendor.

An ORM, by contrast, is an abstraction layer that maps database tables to objects in an object-oriented programming language. Instead of writing SQL, a developer interacts with objects and calls methods (e.g., user.save(), products.all()), and the ORM generates the corresponding SQL automatically. The primary advantage of an ORM is a significant boost in developer productivity and a reduction in boilerplate code. It also provides a level of database independence, as the ORM can often be configured to work with different SQL databases with minimal code changes.

The key comparison points are productivity versus performance. ORMs are generally faster for development, whereas raw SQL offers better performance because queries can be hand-tuned. Another comparison is abstraction versus control. ORMs abstract away the database, making code cleaner and more object-oriented, while raw SQL gives the developer direct control over every aspect of the database interaction.
SQL is for databases. ORM is also for databases but it's different. Developers use them to get data. SQL is harder to write.

Raw SQL is when you write the actual SQL commands yourself, like SELECT * FROM users. This gives you a lot of control over the query. An ORM is a tool that lets you work with the database using programming objects instead of writing SQL. For example, you might write User.find(1) and the ORM will create the SQL for you. An ORM is often faster to develop with because you write less code, but sometimes the SQL it creates is not as efficient as a hand-written query. So, SQL can be faster to run but slower to write.

prompts.question: Assess the risk of an ORM generating inefficient SQL queries and the strategies a developer can use to mitigate this risk.
prompts.scenario: A web application is performing poorly under load. After investigation, the development team discovers that a page which should only run two database queries is running over 100. The lead developer must explain to the team what an 'N+1 query problem' is and assess the strategies they can use to fix it within their ORM.
markerNotes: A strong assessment will make a nuanced judgement about the risk.
Look for a specific, technical example of an ORM-generated inefficiency, with the 'N+1 problem' being the classic case.
The answer must discuss specific mitigation techniques; 'eager loading' is the most direct solution to the N+1 problem.
The conclusion should reflect a mature understanding of the trade-offs involved.
commonStudentErrors: Stating that ORMs are 'bad' without providing a balanced assessment.
Unable to name or describe a specific inefficiency like the N+1 problem.
Not being able to suggest a specific mitigation strategy like 'eager loading'.
Failing to conclude that the developer's understanding is key to managing the risk.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Makes a judgement on the value of the risk, identifying it as significant but manageable (1 mark). • Provides evidence, explaining how an ORM can generate inefficient queries like the 'N+1 problem' (2 marks). • Discusses mitigation strategies such as 'eager loading' (2 marks). • Draws a warranted conclusion about the need for developers to understand the ORM's behaviour (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
The risk of an Object-Relational Mapper (ORM) generating inefficient SQL queries is significant and can lead to severe application performance issues if not properly managed. My assessment is that while the risk is real, it is a manageable trade-off for the productivity gains that ORMs provide.

The most common example of this risk is the 'N+1 query problem'. A developer might write a simple line of code to fetch a list of articles and their authors, for instance. A naive ORM could translate this into one query to fetch N articles, and then N subsequent queries to fetch the author for each article, resulting in N+1 total queries. For a large number of articles, this is extremely inefficient and will cripple database performance.

However, developers can mitigate this risk with several strategies. The primary strategy is to use 'eager loading'. Instead of letting the ORM 'lazily' load the related authors one by one, the developer can explicitly instruct the ORM to fetch all the required authors in a single, efficient JOIN query at the beginning. Most modern ORMs provide methods to easily specify this. Other strategies include using query analysis tools to inspect the SQL generated by the ORM during development and, where absolute performance is critical, dropping down to raw SQL for that specific query while still using the ORM for simpler operations.

In conclusion, while ORMs introduce a performance risk by abstracting away the SQL, this risk is acceptable. The onus is on the developer to not treat the ORM as a magic box, but to understand its behaviour and use available tools and techniques like eager loading to ensure the generated queries remain efficient.
An ORM can make bad SQL that is very slow. This is a risk for the program. The developer can try to fix it by writing better code or maybe using SQL instead for that part.
ORMs are slow. You should use SQL if you want it to be fast.

dotPoints.description: Design, develop and implement a progressive web app (PWA)

prompts.question: Justify two specific UI/UX design choices that would improve the accessibility and inclusivity of a PWA designed to display public transport schedules.
prompts.scenario: A development team is building a new PWA for their city's public transport system. The project brief states that the app must be 'accessible and inclusive'. The lead UX designer asks the team to justify specific design choices that will meet this requirement.
markerNotes: Each justification must be specific and user-centric.
Look for an understanding of the needs of different disability groups (e.g., visual, motor).
Strong answers will propose concrete, technical or design-led solutions (high-contrast mode, keyboard navigation, ARIA labels) rather than vague ideas.
commonStudentErrors: Suggesting generic good design practices without linking them to specific accessibility needs.
Failing to justify the choice from the perspective of a user with a disability.
Proposing a feature but not explaining why it improves accessibility or inclusivity.
Providing only one valid choice.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Proposes a first clear UI/UX choice related to accessibility (1 mark). • Justifies the choice by explaining how it helps a specific group of users (2 marks). • Proposes a second clear UI/UX choice related to accessibility (1 mark). • Justifies the second choice by explaining how it helps a specific group of users (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer:
For a public transport PWA, two justified design choices would be implementing a high-contrast colour mode and ensuring all functionality is keyboard-navigable.

First, I would justify the inclusion of a user-selectable high-contrast mode. The default design might use subtle brand colours, but these can be very difficult to read for users with low vision or colour blindness. A high-contrast mode (e.g., black background with yellow or white text) dramatically increases legibility. This choice is justified because it directly addresses the needs of a significant portion of the population, ensuring that critical information like train times and platform numbers is clearly readable for everyone, thereby improving inclusivity.

Second, I would justify the requirement that every interactive element, from searching for a station to viewing a timetable, must be fully accessible and operable using only a keyboard. This is crucial for users with motor impairments who cannot use a mouse, as well as for screen reader users who rely on keyboard navigation (e.g., using the Tab key) to move through the interface. This choice is justified because it ensures that users who cannot use traditional pointing devices are not excluded from accessing the app's core functionality, which is a fundamental requirement for public service accessibility.

One choice is to make the text bigger so people who can't see well can read it. This is a good idea for a public transport app because lots of different people use it. Another choice is to make sure you can use it with a screen reader. This is justified because blind people need to be able to use the app too.
The app should have good colours and be easy to use for everyone.

prompts.question: Explain the role of service workers in turning a web app into a Progressive Web App (PWA) and how they enable offline functionality.
prompts.scenario: A regional news website wants users to be able to read articles even when they have no internet connection (e.g., on the train). The development team is implementing service workers for the first time.
markerNotes: Must mention background script, caching strategies (Cache API), intercepting fetch events.
commonStudentErrors: Confusing service workers with Web Workers.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Describes service worker lifecycle (2 marks) • Explains caching and fetch interception for offline use (3 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: A service worker is a special JavaScript script that runs in the background, separate from the web page, and acts as a programmable proxy between the web app and the network. During installation, it can precache critical assets (HTML, CSS, JS, images). On subsequent visits, it intercepts network requests via the fetch event and can serve cached responses even when offline. Strategies like 'cache-first' or 'network-first with fallback to cache' allow a PWA to function without internet connectivity, dramatically improving reliability on flaky mobile networks and enabling true app-like behaviour.

dotPoints.description: Apply a web-based database and construct script that executes SQL

prompts.question: Describe the purpose of an SQL INNER JOIN clause. Provide a practical example of a query that uses it to combine data from a Users table and a Posts table.
prompts.scenario: A junior developer is tasked with creating a webpage that displays a list of blog posts and the name of the author for each post. The post content is in a Posts table and the author names are in a Users table. They need to construct a single SQL query to retrieve all the necessary information.
markerNotes: The description must correctly state that it returns only the matching rows from both tables.
The example query must be syntactically correct, including the SELECT, FROM, INNER JOIN, and ON clauses.
The ON condition must correctly link the primary key of one table to the foreign key of the other.
commonStudentErrors: Confusing INNER JOIN with other join types like LEFT JOIN or UNION.
Providing an incorrect or incomplete syntax for the query.
Failing to explain that the join is based on a matching value in a common column.
Using an example with tables that have no logical relationship.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Provides key characteristics of an INNER JOIN, explaining that it combines rows from two tables (1 mark). • Correctly states that the join is based on a matching value in a common column (1 mark). • Provides a syntactically correct SQL query example (1 mark). • The example correctly joins two plausible tables on their primary/foreign key relationship (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: An INNER JOIN is used in SQL to get data from two tables at once. You use it when the tables are related. For example, if you have a Users table and a Posts table, you can join them to find out who wrote which post. The query would look like: SELECT * FROM Posts JOIN Users ON Posts.AuthorID = Users.UserID;
A join puts two tables together.

prompts.question: Distinguish between a WHERE clause and a HAVING clause in an SQL query.
prompts.scenario: A data analyst is trying to write a query to find all customers who have a total order value of more than $1000. They have tried using WHERE SUM(order_value) > 1000 but it gives a syntax error. A senior analyst needs to distinguish between the WHERE and HAVING clauses to explain why their query is failing.
markerNotes: The essential point of distinction is the timing of the filter in relation to the GROUP BY operation.
A strong answer will clearly state that WHERE acts on rows and HAVING acts on groups.
Credit answers that correctly state that HAVING can filter on the results of aggregate functions, while WHERE cannot.
commonStudentErrors: Stating they do the same thing.
Failing to explain the different timing (before vs. after aggregation).
Not mentioning that HAVING is used with GROUP BY and aggregate functions.
Providing an incorrect syntax example.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Indicates the key difference: WHERE filters rows before aggregation, while HAVING filters groups after aggregation (3 marks). • Provides a simple example or notes that HAVING must be used with GROUP BY (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: The key distinction between the WHERE clause and the HAVING clause in SQL is the stage at which they filter data. The WHERE clause is used to filter individual rows from a table before any grouping or aggregation occurs. It operates on the raw data. In contrast, the HAVING clause is used to filter entire groups of rows after they have been created by a GROUP BY clause. It can only be used in conjunction with GROUP BY and it operates on the results of aggregate functions. For example, WHERE sale_amount > 100 would filter out individual sales before they are grouped, while HAVING SUM(sale_amount) > 1000 would filter out entire groups (e.g., a whole customer's sales) only if the total sum for that group meets the criteria.
WHERE is used to filter rows. HAVING is used to filter groups. You use WHERE before GROUP BY, and you use HAVING after GROUP BY. HAVING is for when you are using functions like SUM or COUNT.
They both are for filtering in SQL.

prompts.question: Identify two different types of SQL table joins.
prompts.scenario: A database administrator is explaining to a trainee that there are multiple ways to combine data from two tables, depending on whether you want to include non-matching rows.
markerNotes: The student must simply name two distinct join types. Explanations are not required.
Acceptable answers include: INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, FULL OUTER JOIN.
commonStudentErrors: Naming only one type of join.
Confusing joins with other SQL clauses like WHERE or UNION.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Correctly names one type of SQL join (1 mark). • Correctly names a second, distinct type of SQL join (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: Two different types of SQL table joins are the INNER JOIN and the LEFT JOIN (or LEFT OUTER JOIN).
Inner join and outer join.
Table joins.

prompts.question: A social media platform has three tables: Users (UserID, Username), Posts (PostID, PostContent, AuthorID), and Comments (CommentID, CommentText, PostID, CommenterID). Construct an SQL script that retrieves the CommentText and the Username of the commenter for all comments on a specific post with PostID = 123.
prompts.scenario: A back-end developer needs to create an API endpoint that will display all the comments for a given blog post. To do this, they need to construct an SQL query that retrieves the comment text from one table and the commenter's username from another table.
markerNotes: The query must successfully combine data from two tables (Comments and Users).
The ON clause must correctly link the foreign key (CommenterID) to the primary key (UserID).
The WHERE clause is essential for correctly answering the prompt.
Table aliases are good practice but not essential for marks.
commonStudentErrors: Failing to join the tables, thus being unable to retrieve the Username.
Joining on the wrong columns.
Forgetting the WHERE clause to filter for the specific post.
Selecting the wrong columns in the final output.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Correctly selects the required fields (CommentText, Username) (1 mark). • Correctly uses an INNER JOIN to link the Comments and Users tables on the correct keys (1 mark). • Correctly uses a WHERE clause to filter for the specific PostID (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
sql
SELECT
 c.CommentText,
 u.Username
FROM
 Comments c
INNER JOIN
 Users u ON c.CommenterID = u.UserID
WHERE
 c.PostID = 123;

sql
SELECT CommentText, Username
FROM Comments
JOIN Users ON Comments.CommenterID = Users.UserID
WHERE PostID = 123;
SELECT * FROM Comments WHERE PostID = 123

prompts.question: Explain the purpose of database indexing and evaluate its impact on the performance of a web application that frequently searches a large Users table by email address.
prompts.scenario: A login system is becoming slow because every login triggers a full table scan on a Users table with millions of rows.
markerNotes: Must mention B-tree, reduced search time from O(n) to O(log n), trade-off with write performance.
commonStudentErrors: Thinking indexing speeds up all queries automatically.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Explains what an index is and how it works (2 marks) • Evaluates performance improvement for reads (2 marks) • Mentions trade-offs (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: A database index is a data structure (commonly a B-tree) that provides a quick lookup mechanism for specific columns, dramatically reducing the number of rows the database engine must scan. Without an index, a query like WHERE email = 'user@example.com' requires a full table scan (O(n)). With an index on the email column, lookup becomes O(log n). For a login system with millions of users, this can reduce query time from seconds to milliseconds, vastly improving perceived performance and scalability. However, indexes consume additional storage and slow down INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE operations because the index must be maintained. In a user authentication context where reads far outnumber writes, the performance gain overwhelmingly justifies the minor write overhead.

dotPoints.description: Observe and describe the back-end process used to manage a web request

prompts.question: Describe the lifecycle of a typical web request handled by a back-end web framework, from the moment it's received by the web server to the generation of a response.
prompts.scenario: A junior developer is learning a new back-end framework like Django or Express.js. To understand how the different parts work together, a senior developer asks them to describe the sequence of events that occurs when a user clicks a link to view a product page.
markerNotes: The description must follow a logical sequence: Request -> Routing -> Controller/View Logic -> Model Interaction -> Response.
Look for key terminology: web server, web framework, routing, controller, model, database, template, response.
Higher marks for answers that clearly separate the roles of each component in the framework.
Using a specific example (e.g., a request to '/products/123') can strengthen the answer.
commonStudentErrors: Providing a jumbled or illogical sequence of events.
Failing to use correct terminology like 'routing' or 'controller'.
Confusing the role of the web server with the web framework.
Not mentioning the interaction with the database (model layer).
prompts.markingCriteria: • Describes the initial handling by the web server and passing the request to the framework (1 mark). • Explains the role of the router in matching the URL to a specific controller/function (2 marks). • Describes the controller's role in processing the request and interacting with the model/database (2 marks). • Explains the final step of rendering a template or generating a response to be sent back to the client (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
The lifecycle of a web request begins when a user's browser sends an HTTP request to a specific URL. A web server (like Nginx or Apache) first receives this request. Its role is to handle the raw network connection and forward the request to the application, which is managed by a web framework.

Once the framework receives the request, the first step is routing. The framework's router inspects the URL path (e.g., '/products/123') and matches it against a predefined list of URL patterns to determine which piece of code should handle it. This code is typically a function or method within a 'controller' or 'view'.

The matched controller function is then executed. This is where the core business logic resides. The controller might parse data from the request, like the product ID '123'. It then interacts with the 'model' layer, which is an abstraction over the database. It would ask the model to fetch the details for the product with ID 123.

After the model retrieves the data from the database and returns it to the controller, the controller's final job is to prepare the response. It might pass the product data to a template engine, which generates an HTML document. Finally, the framework wraps this HTML in an HTTP response and sends it back through the web server to the user's browser.

When you request a webpage, the server sends it to the web framework. The framework's router looks at the URL and decides which code to run. That code, the controller, gets information from the database using the model. Then it puts that information into an HTML template and sends the final page back to you.
The server gets a request and sends back a webpage.

dotPoints.description: Describe how collaborative work practices between front-end and back-end developers improve the development of a web solution

prompts.question: Analyse how a well-defined API contract is a crucial collaborative work practice that improves the development of a web solution by front-end and back-end teams.
prompts.scenario: A project is experiencing significant delays because the front-end team is constantly waiting for the back-end team to finish their work before they can begin. A technical lead needs to analyse how establishing a formal API contract at the start of the project could solve this problem and improve collaboration.
markerNotes: The analysis must identify the core problem: dependencies between front-end and back-end teams.
It must explain what an API contract is (a formal agreement on the structure of API requests and responses).
The key benefit to analyse is enabling parallel development.
Credit answers that mention using mock servers or tools like OpenAPI/Swagger.
commonStudentErrors: Describing front-end and back-end roles without discussing the collaborative practice.
Failing to explain what an API contract is.
Not linking the API contract to the key benefit of parallel development.
Providing a vague answer about 'good communication' without specific technical practices.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Identifies the relationship between front-end/back-end teams and their reliance on an API (1 mark). • Examines an API contract as a formal agreement that defines the API's structure and data format (2 marks). • Analyses how this contract enables parallel development by allowing the front-end to work with a mock API (2 marks). • Draws a conclusion about how this reduces integration issues and improves efficiency (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
A well-defined API contract is a critical collaborative practice that dramatically improves web development by decoupling the front-end and back-end teams. The core of the analysis is how it enables parallel development. An API contract is a formal agreement, often documented using a specification like OpenAPI, that defines exactly how the API will behave: what endpoints will be available, what data formats are required for requests, and what the structure of the responses will be.

By establishing this contract upfront, the back-end team can build the API to match the specification, while the front-end team can immediately start development using a 'mock' server that returns dummy data conforming to the same contract. This eliminates the traditional waterfall-style dependency where the front-end must wait for the back-end to be complete. Both teams can work simultaneously, which significantly accelerates the overall project timeline.

Furthermore, this practice reduces integration problems. Because both teams have been building towards the same, unambiguous specification, the final integration of the front-end with the real back-end is much smoother, with fewer surprises and bugs. In conclusion, the API contract acts as the 'single source of truth' that allows two separate teams to work independently but cohesively, leading to a faster and more reliable development process.

An API contract is an agreement between the front-end and back-end teams on what the API will look like. It explains what data to send and what data you get back. This is good because it lets the front-end team start working right away with a fake API, without having to wait for the back-end team to finish. This makes development faster. It also helps because everyone agrees on how it should work, so there are fewer bugs when they put it all together.
Good collaboration means the front-end and back-end developers talk to each other so they can build the website.

dotPoints.description: Develop a web application using an appropriate scripting language with shell scripts to make files and directories, and searching for text in a text file

prompts.question: Construct a shell script command to search for all occurrences of the error code '500' within a web server log file named 'server.log' and save the output to a new file named 'errors.txt'.
prompts.scenario: A system administrator needs to quickly isolate critical server errors from a massive log file to investigate a crash.
markerNotes: Command: grep
Search term: '500'
File: server.log
Redirection: > errors.txt
prompts.markingCriteria: • Correct use of 'grep' command (1 mark) • Correct syntax for search term and source file (1 mark) • Correct use of redirection operator '>' to save to file (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
`grep "500" server.log > errors.txt`

This command uses `grep` to search for the string "500" inside `server.log` and redirects the output (using `>`) to create (or overwrite) a file named `errors.txt` containing only the matching lines.

prompts.question: Explain how a shell script could be used to automate the deployment process of a web application. Include at least two specific tasks the script might perform.
prompts.scenario: A developer is tired of manually typing five different commands every time they want to update the live website. They decide to write a `deploy.sh` script.
markerNotes: Tasks: Git pull, installing dependencies, running tests, restarting server, backing up DB.
Benefit: Consistency, speed, reduced human error.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Explains the purpose of the script (grouping commands for automation) (1 mark) • Describes Task 1 (e.g. pulling code from git) (1 mark) • Describes Task 2 (e.g. restarting web server) (1 mark) • Links to benefit (efficiency/reliability) (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
A shell script automates the deployment by grouping a sequence of necessary terminal commands into a single executable file. This ensures the process is consistent and reduces human error. Two specific tasks might include:
1. **Updating the Codebase:** The script executes `git pull origin main` to download the latest code from the repository.
2. **Restarting Services:** The script executes `systemctl restart nginx` (or similar) to reload the web server so the changes take effect.
By running `./deploy.sh`, the developer performs complex operations reliably in seconds.

dotPoints.description: Distinguish between artificial intelligence (AI) and ML

prompts.question: Distinguish between Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML).
prompts.scenario: A technology journalist is writing an article and asks a software engineer to clarify for their readers whether the terms 'AI' and 'Machine Learning' can be used to mean the same thing.
markerNotes: The response must recognise the hierarchical relationship (ML is a subset of AI).
Look for the distinction between the broad goal (AI) and the specific technique (ML).
Credit answers that correctly identify 'learning from data' as the core characteristic of ML.
commonStudentErrors: Using the terms interchangeably.
Defining one but not the other.
Failing to identify that ML is a subset of AI.
Not clearly explaining that the key difference is 'learning from data' versus being 'explicitly programmed'.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Clearly defines AI as a broad field (1 mark). • Clearly defines ML as a subset of AI that learns from data (1 mark). • Provides a clear point of difference, such as AI being the overall goal and ML being a method to achieve it (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) are related but distinct concepts. AI is the broader field of computer science dedicated to creating machines capable of performing tasks that typically require human intelligence, such as problem-solving, reasoning, and understanding language. AI encompasses a wide range of techniques, including rule-based systems. Machine Learning (ML) is a specific subset of AI. The key distinguishing feature of ML is that instead of being explicitly programmed with rules, an ML system is 'trained' on large datasets to learn patterns and make predictions or decisions on its own. Therefore, the core difference is that AI is the overall goal of simulating intelligence, while ML is a current, primary method for achieving AI by enabling systems to learn from experience.
AI and ML are both about making computers smart like people. AI is a robot and ML is how it learns things. They are basically the same.

Artificial Intelligence is a big area where we try to make smart computers. Machine Learning is part of AI. The difference is that ML is when the computer learns from data by itself, but other types of AI might just follow a lot of 'if-then' rules that a programmer wrote.

prompts.question: Describe a scenario where a software solution would be classified as AI, but not as ML.
prompts.scenario: During a history of computing class, a student asks the teacher to provide an example of an 'intelligent' computer program that existed before the modern era of machine learning.
markerNotes: The key to a good answer is selecting a valid example of a rule-based or symbolic AI system.
The explanation must correctly identify that the system's logic is hard-coded and not learned.
Assess the student's ability to apply the distinction between the two terms to a practical example.
commonStudentErrors: Providing an example of a simple algorithm that is not considered AI (e.g., sorting).
Providing an example that actually does use ML (e.g., a modern chess engine like AlphaZero).
Failing to explain why the example is not ML (the lack of learning from data).
Incorrectly defining the terms.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Describes a valid scenario involving a rule-based or expert system (1 mark). • Clearly explains that the system operates on explicitly programmed rules, not learned patterns (1 mark). • Correctly identifies why this makes it AI (simulates intelligence) but not ML (does not learn from data) (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: A classic example of a software solution that is AI but not ML is a traditional 'expert system' used for medical diagnosis. In this scenario, a vast number of rules, hand-crafted by medical experts, are programmed into the system. For example, 'IF patient has symptom A AND symptom B, THEN there is a high probability of disease X'. The system simulates the reasoning of a human doctor by logically processing these rules against patient data to suggest a diagnosis. This is classified as AI because it performs a task requiring human-like intelligence and reasoning. However, it is not ML because the system does not learn from data. Its knowledge is fixed and explicitly coded by developers. It cannot discover new patterns or improve its diagnostic ability by processing patient outcome data; it can only be improved by a programmer adding or changing the rules manually.
A calculator is AI but not ML. It's smart because it can do math but it doesn't learn.

A chess-playing computer from the 1980s is AI but not ML. It would have a lot of rules programmed into it, like 'if the opponent moves their queen here, then check these possible moves'. It is AI because it seems intelligent, but it is not ML because it is just following rules given by a programmer and not learning how to play better by looking at old games.

prompts.question: Distinguish between Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML), using the development of a chess-playing program as an example.
prompts.scenario: A manager uses the terms 'AI' and 'ML' interchangeably in a project meeting. A software engineer needs to clarify the distinction between the two concepts to ensure the team has a precise understanding of the project's goals.
markerNotes: The core of the distinction is the relationship: ML is a subset of AI.
A strong answer will define AI as the broad concept of creating intelligent machines, and ML as a specific approach to achieving AI by learning from data.
The chess example should clearly illustrate the difference between a rule-based AI and an ML-based AI.
commonStudentErrors: Defining the terms but not explaining the relationship between them.
Stating they are the same thing.
Failing to use the example to illustrate the distinction.
Providing inaccurate definitions.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Correctly defines AI as the broad field of simulating human intelligence (1 mark). • Correctly defines ML as a subset of AI that uses data to learn and make predictions (1 mark). • Clearly indicates the relationship (ML is a way to achieve AI) (1 mark). • Effectively uses the chess example to contrast a rule-based AI with a learning-based ML system (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is the broad, overarching concept of creating machines or software that can simulate human intelligence to perform tasks like problem-solving, reasoning, and understanding language. Machine Learning (ML) is a specific subset or application of AI. It is an approach to achieving AI where, instead of being explicitly programmed with rules, a system is trained on large amounts of data to learn patterns and make predictions or decisions on its own.

To distinguish them using a chess-playing program: An early AI chess program (like Deep Blue) was primarily rule-based. It was programmed with a vast number of chess openings, strategies, and a powerful ability to calculate future moves based on these explicit rules. This is AI, but not ML. In contrast, a modern ML-based chess program (like AlphaZero) is an example of ML. It was not taught the rules of chess strategy. Instead, it was simply told the basic rules of how the pieces move and then learned to play at a superhuman level by playing millions of games against itself, learning from the data of its own successes and failures. Both are AI, but the latter uses ML to achieve its intelligence.

AI is the main idea of making smart computers. ML is one part of AI. The difference is that ML is when the computer learns from data. AI can also be just following a lot of 'if-then' rules. For chess, an AI could be programmed with all the best moves. An ML chess program would learn the best moves by playing lots of games.
AI and ML are both about making computers smart. AI is the name for it and ML is how you do it.

prompts.question: Distinguish between artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML), providing examples to illustrate the relationship.
prompts.scenario: A Year 12 student is writing a major work introduction and wants to correctly position ML within the broader field of AI.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Defines AI as broad goal of intelligent systems (1 mark) • Positions ML as a subset/technique of AI (1 mark) • Provides contrasting examples (rule-based vs learning) (1 mark) • Clear relationship diagrammed in words (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: Artificial Intelligence is the broad goal of creating systems that exhibit intelligent behaviour (reasoning, planning, learning, perception). Machine Learning is a subset/technique of AI where systems improve performance on a task by learning patterns from data rather than being explicitly programmed. Examples: rule-based chess engines = AI but not ML; AlphaGo = AI that uses ML (deep reinforcement learning). ML is currently the dominant approach to achieving AI.

dotPoints.description: Explore models of training ML

prompts.question: Compare supervised and unsupervised learning as models for training machine learning systems. Use a relevant example for each.
prompts.scenario: A data scientist is planning a new machine learning project and needs to explain to the project manager which training model is appropriate for their goal: predicting customer churn versus identifying new market segments.
markerNotes: A correct answer must clearly define 'labelled data' and its role in supervised learning.
The examples provided must be appropriate for the type of learning described.
The comparison should highlight both the different data requirements and the different objectives of the two models.
commonStudentErrors: Confusing the meaning of 'labelled data'.
Providing incorrect or ambiguous examples.
Failing to explain the different goals of each model (prediction vs. discovery).
Not explicitly stating that the type of data (labelled vs. unlabelled) is the primary difference.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Clearly explains supervised learning with a correct example (1 mark). • Clearly explains unsupervised learning with a correct example (1 mark). • Identifies the key difference being the use of labelled vs unlabelled data (1 mark). • Provides a clear point of comparison regarding their different goals (prediction vs pattern discovery) (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: Supervised and unsupervised learning are two primary models for training machine learning systems, differing mainly in the type of data they use and the problems they solve. Supervised learning uses 'labelled' data, meaning each data point in the training set is tagged with a correct output or answer. The goal is to learn a mapping function that can predict the output for new, unseen data. For example, a supervised model for email spam detection is trained on a dataset of emails that have been explicitly labelled as either 'spam' or 'not spam'. In contrast, unsupervised learning uses 'unlabelled' data, where there are no predefined outputs. The goal is for the algorithm to find hidden patterns, structures, or groupings within the data on its own. For example, an e-commerce site might use unsupervised clustering to identify distinct groups of customers based on their purchasing behaviour, without any prior knowledge of what those groups might be. Therefore, the key comparison is that supervised learning predicts outcomes based on known examples, while unsupervised learning discovers inherent structures in the data.
Supervised learning is when a person watches the computer learn. Unsupervised is when it learns by itself. You use it to find things in data.

Supervised learning uses data that has answers. For example, pictures of cats that are labelled 'cat'. Unsupervised learning uses data with no labels. For example, finding groups of customers. The main difference is the labelled data.

prompts.question: Explain the training model of 'reinforcement learning'. Use the example of a game-playing AI to support your answer.
prompts.scenario: A game development company is building an advanced AI opponent for their new strategy game. Instead of scripting its behaviour, they plan to use machine learning. A designer asks the lead AI programmer to explain the concept of reinforcement learning and how it will enable the AI to learn to play the game on its own.
markerNotes: A good explanation must include the key components: agent, environment, actions, and rewards.
The concept of 'trial and error' learning is central to the answer.
The example should clearly identify what constitutes the agent, environment, and rewards in that context.
commonStudentErrors: Confusing reinforcement learning with supervised learning.
Providing a vague explanation without using key terms like 'agent', 'environment', and 'reward'.
Failing to explain that the goal is to maximise cumulative reward over time.
The example is not clearly linked back to the core concepts.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Explains the core concept of an 'agent' learning through 'trial and error' in an 'environment' (1 mark). • Explains the role of 'rewards' and 'penalties' in guiding the agent's learning (2 marks). • Applies the concepts correctly to the example of a game-playing AI (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: Reinforcement learning is a machine learning training model where an 'agent' learns to make a sequence of decisions by interacting with an 'environment'. It is a process of trial and error, where the agent is not given labelled data but instead receives feedback in the form of 'rewards' or 'penalties'. The agent's goal is to learn an optimal 'policy'—a strategy for choosing actions that maximises its cumulative reward over time. For example, in a game-playing AI for a chess game, the AI is the agent and the chessboard is the environment. The AI makes a move (an action), and it receives a positive reward for a good move (like capturing a powerful piece) or a negative reward (penalty) for a bad move (like losing its queen). A large positive reward is given for winning the game, and a large penalty for losing. By playing millions of games against itself and constantly adjusting its policy to favour moves that lead to higher rewards, the AI learns to play chess at a superhuman level without ever being explicitly taught the rules or strategies.

Reinforcement learning is when the computer learns by trying things. If it does something good, it gets a reward. If it does something bad, it gets punished. For a game AI, it gets a reward for winning the game, so it learns how to win.
Reinforcement learning is like training a dog with treats.

prompts.question: Compare supervised and unsupervised learning, describing a suitable application for each.
prompts.scenario: A data scientist is explaining the different approaches to a machine learning problem to a client. They need to compare the two primary training models—supervised and unsupervised learning—and give a clear example of when each would be used.
markerNotes: The comparison must highlight the key difference: the presence or absence of labelled data.
A suitable application for supervised learning (e.g., spam detection, image classification) must be provided.
A suitable application for unsupervised learning (e.g., customer segmentation, anomaly detection) must be provided.
Look for an understanding of the goal of each model (prediction vs. finding structure).
commonStudentErrors: Confusing which model uses labelled data.
Providing inappropriate or vague examples.
Failing to compare the two models directly.
Only describing one of the models.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Clearly explains supervised learning as learning from labelled data to make predictions (1 mark). • Provides a suitable example for supervised learning (1 mark). • Clearly explains unsupervised learning as finding patterns in unlabelled data (1 mark). • Provides a suitable example for unsupervised learning (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
Supervised and unsupervised learning are two primary models of training ML, and they differ mainly in the type of data they use. Supervised learning uses a 'labelled' dataset, where each data point is tagged with a correct output or answer. The goal is to learn a mapping function that can predict the output for new, unseen data. A suitable application is email spam detection. The model is trained on a large dataset of emails that have been pre-labelled as either 'spam' or 'not spam'. The trained model can then classify new, incoming emails.

In contrast, unsupervised learning uses an 'unlabelled' dataset, where there are no predefined outputs. The goal is to find hidden patterns, structures, or clusters within the data itself. A suitable application is customer segmentation for a marketing team. The model would be given customer purchase data and could group customers into distinct clusters based on their buying habits (e.g., 'frequent shoppers', 'high-value purchasers') without any prior labels. The business can then target these segments with different marketing strategies.

The difference is the data you use. Supervised learning uses data that has labels, like pictures of cats that are labelled 'cat'. This is used to predict things, like if a new picture is a cat or not. Unsupervised learning has no labels. It's used to find groups in the data. For example, it could group news articles about sports together without being told what 'sports' is.
Supervised learning is when a person watches the computer learn. Unsupervised is when it learns by itself.

prompts.question: Distinguish between reinforcement learning and semi-supervised learning, providing a practical scenario for each.
prompts.scenario: A data science team has a project with a massive dataset, but only a small fraction of it is labelled. For another project, they need to train an agent to master a game. A junior member of the team needs to understand which training model is appropriate for each scenario.
markerNotes: The key distinction for reinforcement learning is the agent-environment interaction with rewards/penalties.
The key distinction for semi-supervised learning is the use of a mix of labelled and unlabelled data.
Look for appropriate examples (e.g., game AI for reinforcement, text classification with a large corpus for semi-supervised).
commonStudentErrors: Confusing semi-supervised with supervised or unsupervised learning.
Describing reinforcement learning as a type of supervised learning.
Providing vague or incorrect examples for the scenarios.
