Failing to clearly state the point of distinction for each.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Clearly defines reinforcement learning, focusing on the agent-environment-reward loop (1 mark). • Provides a suitable example for reinforcement learning (1 mark). • Clearly defines semi-supervised learning, focusing on the combination of labelled and unlabelled data (1 mark). • Provides a suitable example for semi-supervised learning (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
Reinforcement learning and semi-supervised learning are distinct training models for different problem types. Reinforcement Learning (RL) involves an 'agent' that learns to make a sequence of decisions in an 'environment' to maximise a cumulative 'reward'. The agent learns through trial and error, receiving feedback as either a reward or a penalty for its actions. A practical scenario is training an AI to play a video game like Chess or Go. The agent (the player) makes moves (actions) in the game (environment) and receives a reward (+1) for winning or a penalty (-1) for losing.

Semi-supervised learning, in contrast, is a hybrid approach used for tasks like classification or regression when there is a small amount of labelled data and a large amount of unlabelled data. The model first learns the structure of the data from the entire unlabelled dataset (similar to unsupervised learning) and then uses the small labelled dataset to fine-tune its decision boundaries. A practical scenario is document classification. A company might have millions of documents but only the resources to have a human manually label a few thousand. A semi-supervised model can leverage all the documents to achieve higher accuracy than if it only used the small labelled set.

Reinforcement learning is for training robots or AI in games. The AI tries things and gets a reward if it does something good. Semi-supervised learning is when you have some data with labels and a lot without. You use all of it to train the model.
Reinforcement learning is for robots and semi-supervised is for data.

dotPoints.description: Investigate how machine learning (ML) supports automation through the use of DevOps, robotic process automation (RPA) and business process automation (BPA)

prompts.question: Analyse how machine learning is used to support and enhance DevOps practices.
prompts.scenario: The DevOps team at a large online company is struggling to manage the volume of alerts and log data generated by their microservices architecture. The team lead is asked to analyse how adopting machine learning (AIOps) could help them predict failures and respond more effectively to incidents.
markerNotes: A strong analysis will provide at least two distinct examples of ML application in DevOps (e.g., anomaly detection, CI/CD optimisation).
Look for an understanding of the concept of AIOps.
The answer should focus on how ML adds 'intelligence' to existing automation, rather than just automating simple tasks.
commonStudentErrors: Providing a vague answer like 'it automates things'.
Confusing DevOps with Agile.
Failing to provide specific examples of how ML is applied within a DevOps context.
Not explaining the shift from reactive to proactive/predictive operations.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Identifies the key components: ML and DevOps practices (1 mark). • Examines the relationship, explaining how ML can automate the analysis of logs and telemetry to predict failures (2 marks). • Examines how ML can optimise CI/CD pipelines by identifying risky commits or prioritising tests (2 marks). • Draws a conclusion about ML's role in making DevOps more intelligent and predictive (AIOps) (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: Machine learning is increasingly used to enhance DevOps practices by introducing intelligent automation, a field often called AIOps. The analysis shows its role is to move DevOps from a reactive to a predictive and proactive model. One key application is in monitoring and incident response. An ML model can be trained on vast amounts of system logs and performance metrics. It can learn the pattern of 'normal' system behaviour and automatically detect anomalies that might indicate an impending failure, long before they trigger traditional threshold-based alerts. This allows the operations team to intervene before a system outage occurs. Another application is in the CI/CD pipeline. ML can analyse historical data about code changes and test results to predict the risk associated with a new 'commit'. High-risk changes could be flagged for additional human review or subjected to more extensive automated testing, optimising the allocation of testing resources. The conclusion is that ML supports DevOps by automating the complex analysis required to manage modern, large-scale systems, making the entire development and operations lifecycle smarter, faster, and more resilient.

Machine learning can help DevOps. It can look at all the log files from a server, which is too much for a human to read. The ML can find patterns and see if there is a problem or if the server is going to crash. It can also be used in testing to decide which tests are the most important to run after a code change.
ML makes DevOps better by automating it. It uses AI to do the work.

prompts.question: Define 'Robotic Process Automation' (RPA).
prompts.scenario: An office manager is looking for ways to reduce the amount of time their staff spends on manual data entry. An IT consultant suggests they look into 'RPA', and the manager asks for a simple definition.
markerNotes: The definition must be clear that RPA involves software bots, not physical ones.
Look for the key concepts: 'repetitive, rule-based tasks' and 'mimicking human UI interaction'.
commonStudentErrors: Confusing RPA with physical, industrial robots.
Being too vague about the types of tasks it automates.
Failing to mention that it operates at the UI level.
prompts.markingCriteria: • States the meaning of RPA, referring to software 'bots' (1 mark). • Elaborates by explaining that these bots automate repetitive, rule-based tasks by mimicking human interaction with user interfaces (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: Robotic Process Automation (RPA) is a form of business process automation technology where software programs, or 'bots', are configured to emulate and automate repetitive, rule-based human tasks. Unlike other forms of automation, RPA bots typically interact with systems at the user interface (UI) level, mimicking how a human would click, type, and navigate through applications.
RPA is when you use software robots to do boring office jobs, like copying and pasting data from a spreadsheet into a web form.
It's using robots to automate things.

prompts.question: Explain how machine learning is used to enhance automation in both DevOps and Robotic Process Automation (RPA).
prompts.scenario: A company is looking to improve its software development lifecycle and automate repetitive business tasks. A consultant needs to explain how ML can be applied to both their DevOps pipeline and their RPA initiatives to make them more intelligent.
markerNotes: Look for a clear explanation for both DevOps and RPA contexts.
For DevOps, strong answers will mention predictive analytics for testing, or intelligent monitoring.
For RPA, answers should go beyond simple automation and explain how ML allows for handling unstructured data or making decisions.
commonStudentErrors: Confusing DevOps with general software development.
Describing RPA as just 'robots' without understanding it's software automation.
Providing a generic answer about ML without specific examples for DevOps or RPA.
Only explaining one of the two contexts.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Provides a clear explanation of how ML supports DevOps, with a specific example (e.g., predictive analytics, anomaly detection in logs) (2 marks). • Provides a clear explanation of how ML supports RPA, with a specific example (e.g., processing invoices with OCR, sentiment analysis in customer service bots) (2 marks). • Clearly links ML to the concept of making automation more 'intelligent' or data-driven (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
Machine learning (ML) enhances automation by adding a layer of intelligence and decision-making capabilities to established processes in both DevOps and RPA.

In DevOps, ML supports automation through AIOps (AI for IT Operations). For example, ML models can analyse historical build and test data to predict which code changes are most likely to introduce bugs. This allows the CI/CD pipeline to automatically prioritise or run more intensive tests on high-risk commits, optimising resources and catching errors faster. Furthermore, ML can analyse system logs and performance metrics in real-time to detect anomalies that might indicate an impending system failure, enabling proactive responses far quicker than a human operator could manage.

In Robotic Process Automation (RPA), traditional bots are rule-based and can only handle structured data and predefined processes. ML enhances RPA by giving bots the ability to handle unstructured data and make judgements. For instance, an ML model using Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Optical Character Recognition (OCR) can be integrated into an RPA bot to read and understand invoices that come in various formats, extract the relevant information (like amount and due date), and enter it into a financial system. This moves beyond simple automation to 'intelligent automation'.

In DevOps, ML can be used to improve testing. It can look at old data to predict where new bugs might be. This helps automate the testing process to focus on the riskiest parts. For RPA, which is about software bots, ML helps them do more complex tasks. A normal bot can only copy and paste, but an ML-powered bot could read an email, understand if the customer is angry or happy, and then decide what to do next. So ML makes the automation smarter.
ML helps DevOps and RPA by automating them with AI.

prompts.question: Evaluate the role of machine learning in modern DevOps practices, with specific reference to at least two real-world tools or techniques.
prompts.scenario: A large bank is transitioning to continuous deployment. The DevOps lead proposes integrating ML-based anomaly detection and predictive scaling. Senior management wants an evaluation of whether this is justified.
markerNotes: Strong answers will reference tools such as GitHub Copilot for code review, Prometheus + ML forecasting, or AIOps platforms like Moogsoft.
commonStudentErrors: Confusing general automation with ML-specific contributions.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Clear judgement on value (1 mark) • Two distinct ML-in-DevOps applications explained (4 marks) • Evaluation of benefits vs risks/complexity (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: Machine learning significantly enhances DevOps by moving from reactive to predictive operations (AIOps). Two key examples are predictive autoscaling (e.g., using AWS SageMaker to forecast traffic spikes) and intelligent incident management (e.g., PagerDuty or Splunk using ML to correlate alerts and suggest root causes). The benefits are reduced downtime and faster MTTR, but the risks include model drift and false positives. Overall, for organisations with large-scale systems the benefits far outweigh the complexity.

prompts.question: Distinguish between Robotic Process Automation (RPA) and Business Process Automation (BPA) that incorporates machine learning.
prompts.scenario: A company is automating invoice processing. The CFO is confused why the consultant recommends 'intelligent automation' rather than simple RPA bots.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Defines RPA as rule-based UI automation (1 mark) • Defines ML-enhanced BPA/intelligent automation (1 mark) • Contrasts handling of structured vs unstructured data (1 mark) • Provides practical implication/example (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: RPA uses scripted bots to mimic human actions on existing UIs (rule-based, deterministic). ML-enhanced BPA (Intelligent Process Automation) can handle unstructured data (e.g., OCR + NLP to read varying invoice formats), make decisions (approve/reject based on learned risk), and continuously improve without reprogramming. RPA is faster to deploy for structured repetitive tasks; ML-BPA is required for variability and judgement.

dotPoints.description: Describe types of algorithms associated with ML

prompts.question: Describe the operation of the K-Nearest Neighbour (KNN) algorithm for classification. Use a simple example to illustrate your answer.
prompts.scenario: A student is learning about basic classification algorithms. Their teacher asks them to describe, in simple terms, how the K-Nearest Neighbour algorithm decides which class to assign to a new piece of data.
markerNotes: A good description must explain the entire process: find K neighbours, then take a majority vote.
Look for an understanding of the concept of 'feature space' and distance.
The example provided should be simple and clearly illustrate the voting mechanism.
commonStudentErrors: Failing to explain the role of 'K'.
Not mentioning the 'majority vote' step.
Confusing classification with regression or clustering.
Providing an unclear or incorrect example.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Provides key characteristics of KNN as a supervised, non-parametric algorithm (1 mark). • Describes the process: finding the 'K' nearest data points in the training set (1 mark). • Describes the classification step: using a majority vote among the neighbours to assign a class (1 mark). • Provides a simple, clear example (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: K-Nearest Neighbour (KNN) is a simple, supervised machine learning algorithm used for classification. It operates on the principle that data points with similar features are likely to belong to the same class. To classify a new, unlabelled data point, the algorithm plots it in the 'feature space' along with all the existing training data. It then identifies the 'K' nearest data points (the 'neighbours') from the training set, usually based on Euclidean distance. The final step is a majority vote: the new data point is assigned to the class that is most common among its K neighbours. For example, if K=5 and three of the five nearest neighbours to a new point are 'Class A' and two are 'Class B', the algorithm will classify the new point as 'Class A'.

The KNN algorithm is used for classification. It works by looking at the 'K' closest data points from the training data. For example, if you have a new dot on a graph and K is 3, you find the 3 dots that are closest to it. If two of those dots are blue and one is red, you would classify the new dot as blue because that is the majority.
KNN finds the neighbours of the data.

prompts.question: Distinguish between 'linear regression' and 'logistic regression'.
prompts.scenario: A data science student has two problems: one is to predict the exact temperature tomorrow, and the other is to predict whether it will rain tomorrow (yes/no). They need to understand which type of regression algorithm is suitable for each problem.
markerNotes: The core distinction is that linear regression predicts a continuous value, while logistic regression is used for classification.
A strong answer will mention that logistic regression outputs a probability.
Credit answers that provide a clear example for each.
commonStudentErrors: Stating they are the same.
Failing to explain the different types of output (continuous vs. probability/class).
Not providing examples to illustrate the different use cases.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Indicates the key difference in their outputs (Linear: continuous value vs. Logistic: probability/class) (3 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: The key distinction between linear and logistic regression is the type of output they produce and the problem they are used to solve. Linear regression is used to predict a continuous numerical value. It fits a straight line to the data to model the relationship between variables. For example, it could be used to predict a house's price based on its size. In contrast, logistic regression is used for classification problems. Its output is a probability, a value between 0 and 1, which is then used to predict a discrete class. For example, it could be used to predict the probability that an email is 'spam' or 'not spam'.
Linear regression is for predicting a number, like a price. Logistic regression is for predicting a category, like yes or no.
They are both for regression.

prompts.question: Compare the use of a decision tree versus a neural network for a simple classification task, such as predicting whether a loan application should be approved or denied.
prompts.scenario: A bank is developing a new AI model to automate loan application decisions. The technical team proposes using a deep neural network for maximum accuracy. The legal and compliance team raises concerns, stating that they must be able to explain to a customer exactly why their loan was denied. The team must now compare the proposed model with a more interpretable one, like a decision tree.
markerNotes: A high-scoring comparison must be built around the core trade-off: interpretability vs. complexity/performance.
Look for the use of the terms 'white-box' (decision tree) and 'black-box' (neural network).
The answer should apply the comparison to the specific context of loan approval, where explainability is important.
commonStudentErrors: Only describing one model.
Failing to identify interpretability as the key point of comparison.
Confusing the two models.
Not providing a balanced conclusion about the trade-offs.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Shows how both models can be used for classification (similarities) (1 mark). • Identifies difference/differences, focusing on interpretability vs. complexity (2 marks). • Provides elaboration for the decision tree, explaining its rule-based, 'white-box' nature (2 marks). • Provides elaboration for the neural network, explaining its 'black-box' nature but its ability to find more complex patterns (2 marks). • Draws a conclusion about the trade-off between the two models (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
Both decision trees and neural networks can be used for a classification task like loan approval, but they represent a fundamental trade-off between interpretability and performance.

A decision tree is a 'white-box' model. It works by creating a tree-like structure of simple 'if-then-else' rules based on the input features (e.g., 'IF income > $50k AND credit_score > 650 THEN approve'). The key similarity to a neural network is that it learns these rules from data. However, its main advantage is interpretability. The learned rules are highly transparent and can be easily understood by a human. This is crucial in finance, where regulators may require an explanation for why a loan was denied.

A neural network, on the other hand, is a 'black-box' model. It consists of layers of interconnected nodes that learn complex, non-linear patterns in the data. While it may achieve higher accuracy than a simple decision tree, its reasoning is not transparent. It is very difficult to understand why the network made a particular decision. This lack of interpretability can be a major drawback in regulated industries.

In comparison, the decision tree offers lower complexity and high interpretability, making it suitable for tasks where the reasoning must be explained. The neural network offers higher complexity and potentially higher accuracy, making it suitable for tasks where performance is the sole priority and interpretability is less of a concern. The choice between them is a trade-off between understanding the model's decision and the model's predictive power.

Both can be used to decide if a loan is approved. A decision tree is like a flowchart of rules that is easy to understand. You can see exactly why the decision was made. A neural network is like a black box. It is more complicated and can be more accurate, but you don't know how it made its decision. So the difference is that decision trees are easy to explain, but neural networks can be more powerful.
A decision tree is a tree. A neural network is a net. Both are for AI.

prompts.question: Compare the K-Nearest Neighbour (KNN) algorithm with the Logistic Regression algorithm for a binary classification task.
prompts.scenario: A developer needs to build a model to classify customer emails as 'urgent' or 'not urgent'. They are considering two different algorithms, KNN and Logistic Regression, and need to compare their underlying principles and computational complexity.
markerNotes: The comparison must identify KNN as a non-parametric, instance-based algorithm and Logistic Regression as a parametric, linear model.
A key point of comparison is the 'training' phase: KNN is a 'lazy learner' (no real training), while Logistic Regression learns coefficients.
Another point of comparison is prediction speed: KNN is slow at prediction time, Logistic Regression is fast.
Look for an understanding of how each makes a prediction (voting vs. probability).
commonStudentErrors: Confusing Logistic Regression with Linear Regression.
Incorrectly describing how KNN makes a prediction.
Failing to compare the two directly on points like training or prediction speed.
Stating one is 'better' without providing context or trade-offs.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Clearly explains how KNN works (instance-based, voting) (1 mark). • Clearly explains how Logistic Regression works (learns a boundary, predicts probability) (1 mark). • Provides a point of comparison based on the learning approach (e.g., lazy vs. eager learner) (1 mark). • Provides a point of comparison based on performance/complexity (e.g., prediction speed) (1 mark). • Uses appropriate terminology and structure (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
KNN and Logistic Regression are both used for classification but operate on fundamentally different principles. KNN is a non-parametric, instance-based algorithm. It is considered a 'lazy learner' because it doesn't perform a traditional training phase; it simply stores the entire training dataset. To classify a new data point, it finds the 'K' most similar data points (the nearest neighbours) from the training set and makes a prediction based on a majority vote of their classes. Its complexity is at prediction time, as it must compare the new point to all training points.

Logistic Regression, by contrast, is a parametric, linear model. It is an 'eager learner' because during its training phase, it actively learns the coefficients of a logistic function that best separates the classes. Its goal is to find a decision boundary. To classify a new data point, it simply applies the learned function, which is computationally very fast. The output is a probability (e.g., 85% chance of being 'urgent'), which is then converted to a binary class.

A key point of comparison is the trade-off between model complexity and interpretability. Logistic Regression creates a simple, interpretable linear model, while KNN can capture complex, non-linear boundaries but is harder to interpret and slower at prediction.

The difference is how they learn. Logistic Regression learns an equation during training to separate the two classes. KNN doesn't really learn; it just memorises all the training data. When a new email comes in, KNN looks at the 'K' most similar emails it has seen before and lets them vote on the class. Logistic Regression just plugs the new email's data into its equation to get a probability. Because of this, Logistic Regression is fast at making predictions, while KNN can be slow if the dataset is large.
They are both algorithms that can classify emails. KNN uses neighbours and Logistic Regression uses logic.

dotPoints.description: Investigate common applications of key ML algorithms

prompts.question: Describe how machine learning algorithms are used to enable image recognition.
prompts.scenario: A student is doing a presentation on practical applications of AI and wants to describe the process behind how a smartphone can identify faces in a photo.
markerNotes: The description should cover the key stages: training and inference/prediction.
Must mention the use of a large, labelled dataset of images for training.
Credit answers that mention feature extraction (pixels, edges, shapes) or name a relevant model type (e.g., neural networks).
commonStudentErrors: Giving a very high-level answer like 'it uses an algorithm to see'.
Failing to mention the critical role of the training data.
Not distinguishing between the training phase and the prediction phase.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Describes the need for a large, labelled dataset for training (1 mark). • Describes the training process where the algorithm learns to identify patterns/features from the images (1 mark). • Describes the prediction/inference step where the trained model classifies a new, unseen image (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: Machine learning algorithms enable image recognition by learning to identify patterns from a massive number of example images. The process begins by training a model, often a convolutional neural network (CNN), on a large, labelled dataset. For example, to recognise cats, the model would be fed millions of images, some labelled 'cat' and some labelled 'not cat'. During training, the algorithm learns to identify features at different levels of complexity, starting with simple edges and colours, then combining them into shapes, and eventually into more complex features like eyes, ears, and fur patterns. Once trained, the model can be given a new, unlabelled image. It processes the image, extracts the features it has learned to look for, and makes a prediction (or 'inference') about the probability that the image contains a cat.

To do image recognition, you first get a lot of pictures that are already labelled. You feed these into the machine learning algorithm so it can learn what a cat looks like, for example. Then when you show it a new picture, it can tell you if it's a cat.
It uses an algorithm to look at the pixels in an image to see what it is.

prompts.question: Evaluate the effectiveness of machine learning in image recognition tasks compared to traditional computer vision techniques.
prompts.scenario: A medical imaging startup must decide whether to use classic OpenCV techniques or deep learning for detecting tumours in X-rays.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Traditional CV limitations (2 marks) • Advantages of deep learning/CNNs (2 marks) • Judgement with evidence (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: Traditional computer vision relied on hand-crafted features (SIFT, HOG) and classifiers. Deep learning (CNNs) now dominates, achieving superhuman performance on ImageNet (<3% error vs human ~5%). Advantages: automatic feature learning, transfer learning, robustness. Remaining challenges: adversarial examples, data hunger, explainability. Overall, ML (deep learning) has revolutionised the field.

dotPoints.description: Research models used by software engineers to design and analyse ML

prompts.question: Describe how a decision tree model is used to make a classification. Use the example of a bank deciding whether to approve a loan application.
prompts.scenario: A bank is developing a simple automated system to make an initial assessment of loan applications. A software engineer is explaining to the loan officers how a decision tree model could be used to classify applications as 'low risk' or 'high risk'.
markerNotes: The description must explain the tree-like structure (nodes, branches, leaves).
Must correctly identify that nodes represent questions/features, branches represent answers, and leaves represent the final classification.
The loan example must be used effectively to illustrate the process of traversing the tree.
Credit answers that mention the model is 'interpretable' or 'white box'.
commonStudentErrors: Describing a generic flowchart, not a decision tree used for classification.
Failing to use the loan example correctly.
Not explaining what the nodes, branches, and leaves represent in the model.
Confusing a decision tree with a neural network.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Describes the model as a tree-like structure with nodes, branches, and leaves (1 mark). • Explains that each internal node represents a test on a feature (a question) (1 mark). • Explains that each branch represents the outcome of the test (an answer) and leaves represent the final class label (the decision) (1 mark). • Applies the loan example to show how an application is classified by following a path down the tree (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
A decision tree is a supervised learning model that makes a classification by following a series of 'if-then' questions, organised in a tree-like structure. The model consists of nodes, branches, and leaves. Each internal node represents a question about a specific feature of the data. Each branch coming from a node represents a possible answer to that question. The leaves of the tree represent the final classification or decision.

For a loan application, the root node might ask 'Is the applicant's income > $50,000?'. If the answer is 'Yes', we follow that branch. The next node might ask 'Does the applicant have a criminal record?'. If the answer is 'No', we follow that branch, which might lead to a leaf node labelled 'Low Risk' (loan approved). If the income was less than $50,000, we would have followed the 'No' branch from the root node, which might lead to a different set of questions or directly to a leaf node labelled 'High Risk' (loan denied). To classify a new application, you simply start at the top and follow the path down the tree based on the applicant's data until you reach a leaf, which gives you the final prediction.

A decision tree is like a flowchart. You start at the top with a question. For a loan, the question could be 'Is their income high enough?'. If yes, you go down one path. If no, you go down another. Each path asks more questions until you get to the end, which is the final answer, like 'approve loan' or 'deny loan'.
A decision tree helps you make decisions by looking at the data.

dotPoints.description: Explore models of training ML (supervised, unsupervised, semi-supervised, reinforcement)

prompts.question: Compare supervised and unsupervised learning, recommending which approach would be most suitable for detecting credit-card fraud and justifying your choice.
prompts.scenario: A bank’s data science team must choose a primary ML paradigm for a new fraud detection system.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Characteristics of supervised (2 marks) • Characteristics of unsupervised (2 marks) • Recommendation + justification for fraud context (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: Supervised learning requires labelled data (input + correct output); unsupervised finds hidden patterns in unlabelled data. Fraud detection has historical labelled transactions (fraud/normal), making supervised learning (e.g., random forest or neural nets) the standard approach. Unsupervised (e.g., autoencoders, isolation forest) is useful as a secondary layer for zero-day attacks but alone suffers high false positives. Best practice is a hybrid: supervised primary model + unsupervised anomaly detection.

prompts.question: Explain the concept of reinforcement learning and provide one real-world example where it has been successfully applied.
prompts.scenario: A games development company is exploring AI opponents that improve over time without hand-crafted rules.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Core concept: agent, environment, reward (2 marks) • Trial-and-error learning to maximise reward (1 mark) • Real-world example (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: Reinforcement learning trains an agent by trial-and-error interaction with an environment, receiving rewards/penalties. No labelled data – the agent learns an optimal policy to maximise cumulative reward. Example: AlphaGo/AlphaZero (DeepMind) learned superhuman Go and chess by self-play reinforcement learning.

dotPoints.description: Research models used by software engineers to design and analyse ML (decision trees, neural networks)

prompts.question: Compare decision trees and neural networks in terms of interpretability, performance, and suitability for tabular data versus image data.
prompts.scenario: A data science team is choosing a model for a customer churn prediction project (tabular data) versus a facial recognition system.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Interpretability contrast (1 mark) • Performance/scalability (2 marks) • Data type suitability (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: Decision trees are highly interpretable (if-then rules), fast to train, but prone to overfitting. Neural networks (especially deep) offer superior performance on complex data (images, text) but are black boxes. For tabular data, gradient-boosted trees (XGBoost, LightGBM) usually outperform neural nets; for images/video/audio, convolutional/recurrent neural nets are dominant.

dotPoints.description: Describe types of algorithms associated with ML (linear/logistic regression, KNN)

prompts.question: Explain how K-Nearest Neighbours differs from linear regression and when KNN might be preferred.
prompts.scenario: A junior data scientist is confused why KNN is suggested for a recommendation system instead of linear regression.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Parametric vs non-parametric (2 marks) • Global vs local modelling (1 mark) • Example of when KNN preferred (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: Linear regression fits a global linear function; KNN is non-parametric and makes predictions based on local similarity. KNN excels when decision boundary is highly non-linear and with low-dimensional data; linear regression is better for interpretable linear relationships and large datasets.

subTopics.name: Significance and impact of ML and AI

dotPoints.description: Assess the impact of automation on the individual, society and the environment

prompts.question: Assess the impact of automation, driven by AI and ML, on employment and the economy.
prompts.scenario: A government policy advisor is preparing a brief on the future of work and needs to understand the key arguments regarding the economic and social consequences of increased workplace automation.
markerNotes: The response must make an explicit judgement ('assess').
Look for a balanced argument that considers both positive and negative consequences.
The answer should address both employment (individual level) and the economy (societal level).
Higher marks for answers that discuss the nuances, such as the 'skill shift' rather than just 'job loss', and wealth distribution.
commonStudentErrors: Only focusing on the negative impacts (job loss) without considering the positive aspects (new jobs, productivity).
Making a simplistic judgement without supporting it with balanced arguments.
Discussing employment but failing to address the broader economic impacts like productivity or inequality.
Providing a description rather than an assessment (making a judgement).
prompts.markingCriteria: • Makes a clear judgement on the overall nature of the impact (e.g., transformative, dual-edged) (1 mark). • Provides detailed points for the judgement, discussing both positive and negative impacts on employment (2 marks). • Provides detailed points for the judgement, discussing both positive and negative impacts on the economy (2 marks). • Presents a cohesive, well-structured argument (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
The impact of automation driven by AI and ML on employment and the economy is profoundly transformative and dual-edged, creating both significant opportunities and considerable challenges. In my assessment, while it is a powerful engine for economic growth, it is also a major driver of structural change that society must actively manage.

On employment, automation's most visible impact is job displacement, particularly in roles involving routine manual or cognitive tasks, such as factory work, data entry, and customer service. This can lead to unemployment and economic hardship for individuals whose skills become obsolete. However, automation also creates new jobs that require different skills, particularly in areas like data science, AI development, and robotics maintenance. The net effect is a significant 'skill shift' in the workforce, where the demand for creativity, critical thinking, and technical expertise increases.

For the economy, the impact is largely positive in terms of productivity and efficiency. Automation allows businesses to produce more goods and services at a lower cost, leading to economic growth and potentially lower prices for consumers. It enables new business models and innovations that were previously impossible. However, a significant negative ramification is the potential for increased economic inequality. If the productivity gains from automation primarily benefit the owners of capital (e.g., company shareholders) rather than labour (e.g., workers through higher wages), it can lead to a wider gap between the rich and the poor.

Therefore, the overall impact is one of massive disruption. The value to the economy is immense, but this comes with the significant social cost of workforce displacement and the risk of exacerbating inequality if not managed through investment in education, retraining programs, and appropriate social policies.
Automation means robots take people's jobs. This is bad for people because they lose money. But it's good for the economy because companies make more stuff.

Automation has a big impact on jobs. It gets rid of some jobs, like on an assembly line, because robots can do the work. But it also creates new jobs, like programming the robots. For the economy, it's good because companies can be more productive and make more money. This can be a problem though, because it can make some people very rich while others lose their jobs. So the impact is both good and bad.

prompts.question: Evaluate the impact of AI-powered assistive technologies on the lives of people with disabilities.
prompts.scenario: A charitable foundation that supports people with disabilities is considering funding a new software project. They ask an accessibility consultant to prepare a report evaluating the overall impact that recent advancements in AI have had on the disability community.
markerNotes: A top-tier evaluation will provide specific, named examples of modern AI assistive tech (e.g., Seeing AI, voice assistants, live captioning).
The answer should be structured around the impact on the individual, using criteria like independence, communication, and access.
A nuanced conclusion may briefly acknowledge limitations or future challenges.
commonStudentErrors: Providing a generic answer without specific examples of AI-powered assistive technologies.
Describing the technologies but not evaluating their impact on users' lives.
Failing to consider a range of different disabilities.
Not making a clear final judgement.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Makes a clear evaluative judgement on the impact (e.g., 'profoundly positive and empowering') (1 mark). • Applies multiple criteria, such as independence, communication, and access to information (3 marks). • Provides comprehensive evidence for each criterion, using specific examples of AI-powered technologies (e.g., screen readers, voice assistants, image recognition) (3 marks). • Synthesises the evaluation into a nuanced conclusion, perhaps acknowledging potential limitations (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
The impact of AI-powered assistive technologies on the lives of people with disabilities has been profoundly positive and empowering, representing a significant step forward in digital inclusivity. This judgement is based on the criteria of improved independence, communication, and access to the world.

For users with visual impairments, AI-powered tools have been transformative. Modern screen readers use AI to describe images on a webpage, providing context that was previously unavailable. Mobile apps like Seeing AI can use a phone's camera to read text from documents, identify products, and even recognise people, granting a level of independence in daily tasks that was previously unimaginable.

For users with motor impairments, AI-driven voice assistants and speech-to-text technologies have dramatically improved communication and control over their environment. A person who cannot use a keyboard or mouse can now write documents, send messages, and control smart home devices using only their voice. This provides a vital channel for communication, creativity, and self-expression.

For users with hearing impairments, AI-powered automatic captioning on platforms like YouTube and in video conferencing tools provides real-time access to spoken content, enabling participation in education, work, and social life.

In conclusion, while limitations such as cost and accuracy still exist, my evaluation is that the overall impact of these AI technologies is overwhelmingly positive. They are not merely conveniences; they are powerful tools that break down long-standing barriers, fostering independence and enabling fuller participation in society.

The impact of AI on people with disabilities is very good. For example, a blind person can use an app on their phone that uses AI to tell them what it sees through the camera. This helps them be more independent. For a person who can't type, AI can turn their speech into text so they can write an email. These technologies make life much easier and help people do things they couldn't do before. So the impact is very effective.
AI helps people with disabilities by making computers easier to use for them.

prompts.question: Outline two potential impacts of automation on the 'nature and skills required for employment'.
prompts.scenario: A careers advisor is preparing a presentation for high school students about the future of work. They need to outline the key ways that automation is expected to change the job market.
markerNotes: Look for two distinct points.
A good answer will touch on both the decline of some skills (routine tasks) and the rise of others (creative/technical skills).
This question requires a brief outline, not a deep explanation.
commonStudentErrors: Only mentioning job loss.
Being too vague and not specifying the types of skills or tasks affected.
Repeating the same idea twice.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Outlines one valid impact (e.g., displacement of routine tasks) (1 mark). • Outlines a second, distinct impact (e.g., increased demand for higher-order skills) (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: Firstly, automation will lead to the displacement of jobs that are primarily composed of routine, predictable tasks, such as data entry or assembly line work. Secondly, this will simultaneously increase the demand for employees with higher-order skills that are difficult to automate, such as critical thinking, creativity, complex problem-solving, and managing AI systems.
One impact is that robots will take the boring jobs. Another impact is that people will need to learn how to work with the robots.
Robots will take all the jobs.

prompts.question: Discuss the social and economic ramifications of large-scale automation in the logistics industry (e.g., automated warehouses and delivery drones), considering its impact on employment, production efficiency, and wealth distribution.
prompts.scenario: A government committee is studying the future of the Australian economy. They are holding a hearing on the impact of automation and have invited a technology ethicist to discuss the likely social and economic consequences of the widespread adoption of automated systems in the shipping and logistics sector.
markerNotes: A high-scoring answer must provide a balanced discussion that covers all three specified areas of impact.
Look for an understanding of the concept of 'structural unemployment' and the 'skills gap'.
The discussion of wealth distribution should consider the different impacts on consumers, company owners (capital), and workers (labour).
The conclusion should be nuanced and summarise the complex trade-offs.
commonStudentErrors: Only focusing on one ramification (e.g., job loss).
Failing to provide a balanced discussion of both pros and cons.
Not addressing all three aspects mentioned in the question (employment, efficiency, wealth distribution).
Providing a generic answer about automation without linking it to the specific context of the logistics industry.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Identifies the key issues related to automation in logistics (1 mark). • Develops points for the impact on employment (job displacement vs. new roles) (2 marks). • Develops points for the impact on production efficiency (faster, cheaper delivery) (2 marks). • Develops points for the impact on wealth distribution (benefits to consumers/capital owners vs. potential wage depression) (2 marks). • Provides a balanced, nuanced treatment of the overall ramifications (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
Large-scale automation in the logistics industry presents a complex web of social and economic ramifications, creating both clear benefits in efficiency and significant challenges for employment and wealth distribution.

From an economic and efficiency perspective, the impact is overwhelmingly positive. Automated warehouses using robots can operate 24/7 with greater speed and accuracy than manual processes, reducing costs and order fulfilment times. The advent of delivery drones promises to further reduce the cost and time of 'last-mile' delivery. For consumers, this translates to faster, cheaper shipping, and for businesses, it means lower operational costs and higher productivity.

However, the social ramifications for employment are profound. Automation directly displaces large numbers of workers in roles like truck driving, forklift operation, and packing. While automation also creates new, higher-skilled jobs in robotics maintenance, logistics management, and software development, there is a significant risk of a 'skills gap', where the displaced workers do not have the training to transition to these new roles, leading to structural unemployment.

This leads to a discussion of wealth distribution. The productivity gains from automation can lead to lower prices for consumers and higher profits for the owners of the logistics companies (capital). However, if this is accompanied by downward pressure on wages or large-scale unemployment for the former workforce (labour), it can exacerbate economic inequality. The societal benefit of cheaper goods may be offset by the social cost of a displaced workforce.

In conclusion, the issue is complex. While automation in logistics drives significant economic efficiencies, it also poses a major challenge to social equity. The discussion highlights the need for societal investment in retraining and education programs to manage the workforce transition and ensure the economic benefits are distributed broadly.

Automation in logistics, like Amazon's warehouses, has good and bad impacts. It is good for efficiency because robots can pack and move things very fast, which means you get your packages quicker. The bad impact is on jobs. Many people who used to work as drivers or in warehouses will lose their jobs. This can also affect wealth distribution because the company owners get richer from the efficiency, but the workers lose their income. So it helps the economy but can hurt society.
Robots will take all the truck drivers' jobs. This is bad for them.

prompts.question: Assess the impact of automation on the nature and skills required for employment.
prompts.scenario: A government policy advisor is writing a report on the future of the workforce. They need to assess how automation, driven by AI and ML, is changing not just which jobs are available, but the very nature of work and the skills that will be valued in the future.
markerNotes: The assessment must make a clear judgement about the significance of the impact.
It should consider both negative impacts (job displacement) and positive impacts (job creation, augmentation).
A strong answer will identify the shift from routine/manual skills to skills like creativity, critical thinking, and social-emotional intelligence.
Look for a nuanced argument that avoids simplistic 'robots are taking all jobs' claims.
commonStudentErrors: Only focusing on job losses without considering job creation or augmentation.
Providing a list of impacts without making a judgement (assessing).
Failing to identify the specific types of skills that are becoming more or less valuable.
Making overly dramatic and unsupported claims.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Makes a clear judgement on the transformative nature of automation's impact on employment (1 mark). • Provides evidence for the judgement by discussing the displacement of routine and manual jobs (2 marks). • Balances this by discussing the creation of new jobs and the augmentation of existing ones (2 marks). • Concludes by assessing the shift in required skills towards creativity, problem-solving, and other 'human' skills (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
The impact of automation on employment is profoundly transformative; my assessment is that it is causing a fundamental shift in the skills required, rather than simply a net loss of jobs. It is making routine skills obsolete while simultaneously increasing the value of uniquely human skills.

Primarily, automation is displacing jobs that are characterised by routine, repetitive tasks, both manual (e.g., assembly line work) and cognitive (e.g., data entry, basic bookkeeping). These roles are easily broken down into rules that algorithms and machines can execute more efficiently and accurately than humans. This leads to significant job displacement in certain sectors.

However, automation also creates new jobs and augments existing ones. It creates new roles directly related to developing, maintaining, and managing these automated systems (e.g., AI specialists, robotics engineers). More importantly, it augments the roles of many professionals. For example, a doctor's diagnostic abilities can be enhanced by an AI that analyses medical scans, freeing up the doctor to focus on patient interaction, complex case analysis, and treatment planning.

This leads to the crucial shift in required skills. As machines handle the routine, the skills that become most valuable are those that are difficult to automate: critical thinking, complex problem-solving, creativity, emotional intelligence, and collaboration. The future of work will not be about competing with machines, but about collaborating with them, requiring a workforce that is adaptable and skilled in these higher-order cognitive and social abilities.

Automation has a big impact on jobs. It gets rid of jobs that are repetitive, like factory work or data entry, because robots and AI are better at those tasks. This is a negative impact. However, it also creates new jobs, like people who have to program the AI. It also changes jobs. For example, a graphic designer might use AI to help them come up with ideas. This means that skills like creativity and problem-solving are becoming more important than skills that involve doing the same thing over and over.
Automation is bad for employment because robots are taking over all the jobs from people.

prompts.question: Evaluate the environmental impact of developing and running large-scale machine learning models, considering both potential negative and positive effects.
prompts.scenario: A technology company is promoting its new, state-of-the-art AI model as a tool to solve environmental problems. An environmental watchdog group is asked to evaluate this claim, considering not only the potential benefits of the AI's application but also the environmental cost of the AI itself.
markerNotes: The evaluation must be balanced, covering both positive and negative impacts.
For negative impacts, look for mention of high energy consumption and carbon footprint of data centres for training.
For positive impacts, look for specific applications like climate change modelling, optimising energy grids, or reducing waste in supply chains.
A strong answer will make a final, nuanced judgement about the net effect.
commonStudentErrors: Only focusing on the positive applications of AI.
Only focusing on the negative energy consumption aspect.
Failing to make a final judgement or evaluation.
Providing vague examples without explaining the impact.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Makes a clear evaluative judgement about the dual nature of AI's environmental impact (1 mark). • Provides evidence for the negative impact, focusing on the energy required for training large models (2 marks). • Provides evidence for the positive impact, giving specific examples of how AI can be used for environmental benefit (2 marks). • Synthesises these points into a conclusion about the need for responsible development (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
The environmental impact of large-scale machine learning is a significant and dual-sided issue. My evaluation is that while AI presents a major environmental cost, its potential as a tool for optimisation and discovery offers a credible path to solving environmental challenges, making its responsible development crucial.

The primary negative impact is the immense energy consumption required to train and run large-scale models. Modern deep learning models are trained on massive datasets using powerful GPU clusters in data centres for days or weeks at a time. This process has a substantial carbon footprint, consuming vast amounts of electricity which may come from non-renewable sources, and also requires significant water resources for cooling.

Conversely, AI's positive impact lies in its application to complex environmental problems. ML models can be used to make energy grids more efficient by predicting demand and optimising supply from renewable sources. They can analyse satellite imagery to monitor deforestation in real-time, help create more accurate climate change models, and optimise logistics and supply chains to significantly reduce fuel consumption and waste.

In conclusion, while the development of AI itself poses a direct environmental burden, this must be weighed against its potential as a powerful tool to address the very same environmental crises. The challenge for the industry is to mitigate the former, through developing more efficient algorithms and using green energy for data centres, while maximising the latter.

AI has good and bad environmental impacts. The bad impact is that training a big AI model uses a huge amount of electricity in data centres, which causes a lot of pollution. The good impact is that you can use AI to solve environmental problems. For example, AI can help make a power grid smarter to save energy, or it can be used to find ways to reduce waste in factories. So it's a trade-off.
AI can be used to help save the environment.

prompts.question: Assess the positive and negative impacts of workplace automation driven by AI/ML on employment, skills, and economic inequality.
prompts.scenario: A government taskforce is drafting policy on the future of work in an increasingly automated economy.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Positive impacts with examples (3 marks) • Negative impacts with examples (3 marks) • Overall judgement and policy implications (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: Positive: increased productivity, safer conditions (robots in dangerous jobs), new roles (ML engineers). Negative: displacement of routine jobs, skill-biased technological change increasing inequality, potential technological unemployment. Overall assessment: net positive for GDP but requires reskilling programs and policy intervention to avoid widening inequality.

prompts.question: Evaluate the environmental impact of training large language models versus the efficiency gains they enable.
prompts.scenario: An environmental NGO is campaigning about the carbon footprint of generative AI.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Quantifies training impact (2 marks) • Discusses downstream efficiency gains (2 marks) • Balanced judgement (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: Training GPT-3 emitted ~552 tonnes CO₂e (equivalent to 120 cars/year). However, deployed models can reduce emissions through optimisation (e.g., Google reduced cooling energy 40% with DeepMind). Net impact depends on usage – inference-heavy consumer models may have higher lifetime footprint than industrial optimisation use-cases.

dotPoints.description: Investigate the effect of human and dataset source bias in the development of ML and AI solutions

prompts.question: Explain how dataset source bias can lead to unethical outcomes in an AI solution. Provide a relevant example to support your answer.
prompts.scenario: A tech company is facing public criticism after their new AI-powered loan approval system was found to be disproportionately denying applications from minority groups. The lead AI ethicist must explain to the board how this could have happened, even though the algorithm itself contained no explicitly discriminatory rules.
markerNotes: The explanation must connect the biased data to the model's behaviour.
The model's behaviour must then be linked to a specific, unethical real-world consequence.
The example should be clear and directly support the explanation.
Credit answers that use terms like 'representation', 'discrimination', or 'fairness'.
commonStudentErrors: Defining bias but not explaining how it leads to an outcome.
Providing a vague or irrelevant example.
Confusing dataset bias with other forms of AI error.
Failing to explicitly state why the outcome is unethical.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Clearly explains the concept of dataset source bias (1 mark). • Explains how this bias leads to flawed or unfair model behaviour (1 mark). • Links the flawed behaviour to a specific unethical outcome (1 mark). • Provides a clear, relevant example that illustrates the entire process (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: Dataset source bias occurs when the data used to train a machine learning model is not a representative sample of the real-world population or problem space. This flawed data teaches the model to replicate and often amplify existing human or societal biases. This leads to unethical outcomes because the AI solution will make decisions that are systematically unfair or discriminatory against certain groups. The model appears to be objective, but its foundation is biased, resulting in automated, large-scale discrimination. For example, if an AI tool for screening job applicants is trained on a company's historical hiring data, and that data predominantly shows successful applicants being male, the model will learn to associate male characteristics with success. It will then be more likely to reject qualified female applicants, not because of their skills, but because they do not fit the biased pattern in the training data. This is unethical as it perpetuates gender discrimination in hiring.
Bias is when the computer is not fair. If you give it bad data, it will do bad things. For example, it might not like some people. This is unethical.

Dataset bias is when the data you use to train an AI is wrong or unfair. The AI then learns from this bad data and makes unfair decisions. An example is a face recognition system that was trained mostly on pictures of white people. When it is used in the real world, it might not be able to recognise people with darker skin tones accurately. This is unethical because the system doesn't work for everyone.

prompts.question: Recommend three strategies a software engineering team could implement to mitigate the risk of human bias influencing an AI solution.
prompts.scenario: Following an incident where a company's AI model showed bias, the CEO has mandated that all future AI projects must include a 'Bias Mitigation Plan'. A project manager asks the lead data scientist to recommend key strategies for this plan.
markerNotes: Each recommendation must be a distinct, actionable strategy.
The supporting reasons must clearly explain how the strategy helps to mitigate bias.
Look for a comprehensive answer that considers multiple stages of the development lifecycle (data, testing, team composition).
commonStudentErrors: Providing vague recommendations like 'be more careful'.
Recommending only one or two strategies.
Failing to provide a supporting reason for each recommendation.
Focusing only on data and ignoring the role of the development team or testing processes.
prompts.markingCriteria: • States three clear, distinct recommendations (3 marks). • Provides a supporting reason for each recommendation, linking it to the mitigation of bias (3 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: To mitigate the risk of human bias influencing an AI solution, I would recommend three key strategies. Firstly, the team must prioritise diverse and representative data collection. This involves actively seeking out and including data from minority or underrepresented groups in the training set to ensure the dataset mirrors the real-world population. This is supported by the reason that it directly counteracts source bias, preventing the model from learning skewed patterns. Secondly, I recommend implementing regular algorithmic audits and fairness checks. This involves using specialised tools to test the model's predictions across different demographic groups to identify and quantify any discriminatory behaviour before deployment. The reason for this is to establish a continuous process of verification, ensuring that bias has not inadvertently crept into the model. Finally, I recommend fostering diversity within the software engineering team itself. A team with varied backgrounds, cultures, and experiences is more likely to recognise and question potential sources of bias during the design and development process. This is supported by the reasoning that a homogenous team may have shared blind spots, making them less likely to foresee how their solution could negatively impact different communities.
First, use better data that isn't biased. Second, test the AI to see if it is biased. Third, the programmers should be careful not to be biased. These will help fix the problem.
You need to make the computer not biased. Tell it to be fair.

prompts.question: Evaluate the effectiveness of technical strategies (e.g., algorithmic audits, re-sampling data) versus non-technical strategies (e.g., diverse hiring, ethical review boards) in mitigating bias in AI solutions.
prompts.scenario: A major tech company has created an AI ethics task force after one of its products was found to be biased. The task force is debating where to focus its efforts. One group argues for investing in new algorithmic tools to detect and fix bias, while the other argues for investing in organisational change, such as diversity training and ethical review processes. The head of the task force asks for an evaluation of both approaches.
markerNotes: A top-tier evaluation must be nuanced and conclude that a holistic approach is best.
The answer should be structured to systematically evaluate each type of strategy, discussing its strengths and weaknesses.
Look for specific, named examples of each type of strategy (e.g., re-sampling, algorithmic audits, diverse hiring, ethics boards).
The synthesis in the conclusion is critical for the highest marks.
commonStudentErrors: Only describing the strategies without evaluating their effectiveness.
Failing to make a final, comparative judgement.
Arguing for one type of strategy over the other without acknowledging their complementary nature.
Not providing specific examples for both technical and non-technical strategies.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Makes a clear evaluative judgement that both are necessary and complementary (1 mark). • Applies multiple criteria, systematically evaluating technical strategies, explaining their effectiveness but also their limitations (3 marks). • Systematically evaluates non-technical strategies, explaining their effectiveness but also their limitations (3 marks). • Provides comprehensive evidence, using specific examples for each type of strategy (2 marks). • Synthesises the evaluation into a nuanced conclusion about a holistic approach (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: To fix bias, you can use technical or non-technical strategies. A technical strategy is to fix the data, like adding more pictures of people from different groups so the training data is balanced. A non-technical strategy is to hire more diverse people on the team, because they might notice a problem that other people don't see. Both are effective. You probably need to do both to really solve the problem. One fixes the code and the other fixes the people making the code.
You need to hire good people and they will use good code to make the AI not biased.

prompts.question: Analyse how dataset source bias can lead to the development of a biased facial recognition system that performs poorly for certain demographic groups.
prompts.scenario: A tech company has released a new facial recognition system, but reports soon emerge that it is much less accurate at identifying women and people of colour. An ethicist is asked to analyse how this could have happened, focusing on the data the system was trained on.
markerNotes: The analysis must connect the training data to the model's performance.
A strong answer will explain that ML models learn patterns from data, so biased data leads to a biased model.
The facial recognition example must be used to explain the specific consequence: underrepresentation in the training set leads to poor performance for the underrepresented group.
Look for an understanding of the relationship: 'garbage in, garbage out'.
commonStudentErrors: Stating that the AI is 'racist' without explaining the technical cause.
Failing to connect the biased outcome to the biased input data.
Describing bias in general terms without applying it to the specific scenario.
Not explaining *why* an unbalanced dataset causes poor performance.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Identifies the key relationship: the training data is the source of the model's knowledge (1 mark). • Examines the concept of dataset source bias, where the training data is not representative of the real-world population (2 marks). • Analyses the specific consequence in the facial recognition scenario: the model becomes highly specialised in the overrepresented group and lacks accuracy for underrepresented groups (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer:
Dataset source bias is a primary cause for failures in facial recognition systems. The analysis begins with the principle that a machine learning model knows nothing about the world except for the data it is trained on. If the training data is not representative of the population it will be used on, the model's performance will be biased.

In the case of a facial recognition system, dataset source bias occurs if the training dataset is overwhelmingly composed of images of one demographic group (e.g., white males). The model, in learning to minimise its error on this data, becomes an expert at identifying the features and patterns common to that specific group. Consequently, it learns very little about the features and variations present in the faces of underrepresented groups (e.g., women, people of colour).

When this biased model is deployed in the real world, it performs poorly for these underrepresented groups because it has never been properly trained to recognise them. It is not that the AI is inherently biased; rather, it has accurately learned from biased data. The analysis shows a direct causal link: a biased dataset results in a model that amplifies and perpetuates that bias, leading to a system that is not only inaccurate but also ethically problematic and unfair.

This happens because of the data used to train the AI. If the developers trained the facial recognition system mostly on pictures of white men, the AI will get very good at recognising white men. But it won't be good at recognising women or people of colour because it hasn't seen enough examples. So, the source of the data was biased because it wasn't balanced. The result is a biased AI that doesn't work for everyone.
The AI is biased because the people who programmed it were biased. They didn't make it work for everyone.

prompts.question: Analyse how bias in training data led to real-world harm in at least two deployed AI systems and propose mitigation strategies software engineers can employ.
prompts.scenario: A government regulator is reviewing past AI failures to create new guidelines.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Two clear real-world examples with harm explained (4 marks) • Practical mitigation strategies (3 marks) • Conclusion on engineer responsibility (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: Examples: COMPAS recidivism algorithm (biased against African-Americans due to historical arrest data); Amazon hiring tool (penalised resumes with 'women’s'). Mitigations: audit datasets for balance, use fairness constraints (e.g., demographic parity), adversarial debiasing, transparent documentation (model cards, datasheets for datasets).

prompts.question: Explore, through implementation, how cultural bias in a dataset can affect sentiment analysis performance for different dialects or languages.
prompts.scenario: A global social media company discovers its English-trained sentiment model misclassifies sarcasm in Australian slang.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Demonstrates performance drop (3 marks) • Explains cause (cultural/dialect features) (2 marks) • Suggests mitigation (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer: Student trains a sentiment classifier on English movie reviews, then tests on African American Vernacular English (AAVE) tweets – accuracy drops dramatically due to vocabulary differences. Mitigation: include diverse data sources, use domain adaptation techniques.

dotPoints.description: Explore by implementation how patterns in human behaviour influence ML and AI software development

prompts.question: Analyse how psychological principles, such as variable reinforcement schedules, are implemented in AI-driven social media feeds to influence user behaviour and increase engagement.
prompts.scenario: A digital wellbeing advocate is preparing a presentation on the addictive nature of social media. They need to analyse how the AI algorithms that power news feeds are not neutral, but are instead designed using psychological principles to keep users on the platform for as long as possible.
markerNotes: The analysis must connect a specific psychological principle to a specific feature of an AI-driven feed.
A strong answer will explain what a variable reinforcement schedule is (unpredictable rewards).
It should then explain how the AI's content selection (showing a highly engaging post at unpredictable intervals) acts as this variable reward.
The conclusion should link this implementation to the intended outcome (increased user engagement, longer session times).
commonStudentErrors: Stating that social media is 'addictive' without a technical or psychological explanation.
Failing to explain the concept of variable reinforcement.
Not linking the algorithm's behaviour to the psychological principle.
Describing the AI in general terms without focusing on the behavioural influence aspect.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Identifies the key components: the AI algorithm and a relevant psychological principle (1 mark). • Examines the principle of variable reinforcement (delivering rewards unpredictably) (2 marks). • Analyses how the AI feed implements this by curating content to provide unpredictable, highly engaging 'rewards' (posts) (2 marks). • Draws a conclusion about the goal of this design, which is to maximise user engagement and time on site (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
AI-driven social media feeds are engineered to maximise user engagement by implementing psychological principles, most notably variable reinforcement schedules. This principle, discovered through experiments with slot machines, shows that behaviour is most strongly conditioned when rewards are delivered unpredictably.

The AI algorithm that powers a news feed analyses a user's past behaviour (likes, shares, comments, viewing time) to build a detailed profile of what they find engaging. It then curates a feed where highly engaging or rewarding content (e.g., a photo from a close friend, a viral video, a controversial post) is interspersed with less engaging content. The user does not know when the next 'reward' will appear. The act of pulling down to refresh the feed is analogous to pulling the lever on a slot machine. Sometimes the refresh yields nothing interesting, but other times it provides a rewarding piece of content. This unpredictability is what creates a powerful compulsion to keep checking and scrolling.

Therefore, the AI is not simply showing users what they like; it is strategically timing the delivery of that content to exploit a known psychological vulnerability. The analysis shows that the software is not a neutral content-sorter, but an active participant in shaping user behaviour to achieve the business goal of maximising time on the platform.
The AI knows what you like so it shows you things to make you stay on the app.

dotPoints.description: Design, develop and apply ML regression models using an OOP to predict numeric values

prompts.question: Describe the purpose of a linear regression model and outline, using pseudo-code, how a software engineer might structure a simple `LinearRegression` class in an object-oriented programming language.
prompts.scenario: A software engineering student is learning how to implement machine learning models from scratch. Their task is to create a simple linear regression model using object-oriented principles to predict house prices based on their size.
markerNotes: The purpose must be clearly stated: to model the linear relationship between a dependent and independent variable to predict a continuous numeric value.
The OOP structure should be logical, typically with methods for `train` (or `fit`) and `predict`.
The pseudo-code should show attributes for storing the model parameters (slope and intercept).
Look for a logical separation of concerns in the class design.
commonStudentErrors: Confusing regression (predicting a number) with classification (predicting a category).
Providing non-OOP pseudo-code.
Failing to include distinct methods for training and prediction.
Not mentioning the key model parameters (slope/intercept).
prompts.markingCriteria: • Clearly describes the purpose of linear regression (to predict a continuous value based on a linear relationship) (2 marks). • Designs a class with appropriate attributes (e.g., slope, intercept) (1 mark). • Includes a `train` or `fit` method in the pseudo-code to learn the attributes from data (1 mark). • Includes a `predict` method in the pseudo-code to make a prediction for new data (1 mark).

sampleAnswers.answer:
Linear regression is used to predict a number, like a house price, by drawing a straight line through the data points.

An OOP class would have a function to train the model, which finds the line. It would also have a function to make a prediction using that line.

`CLASS LinearRegression:`
  `slope, intercept`

  `FUNCTION train(data):`
    `// find slope and intercept`

  `FUNCTION predict(new_input):`
    `RETURN slope * new_input + intercept`
Linear regression is for predicting things with a line.

prompts.question: Distinguish between Linear Regression and Polynomial Regression. Describe a scenario where Polynomial Regression would be a more suitable model.
prompts.scenario: A data analyst is trying to predict a company's revenue based on its advertising spend. They first try a Linear Regression model, but find it does not fit the data well. A senior analyst suggests they try Polynomial Regression instead and needs to explain why.
markerNotes: The key point of distinction is the shape of the relationship they model: a straight line versus a curve.
A strong answer will explain that Polynomial Regression is still a type of linear model because it is linear in its parameters.
The scenario described should clearly involve a non-linear relationship between the variables.
Credit answers that mention the risk of overfitting with high-degree polynomials.
commonStudentErrors: Stating they are completely different types of models.
Failing to explain that Polynomial Regression fits a curve to the data.
Providing a scenario that is clearly linear.
Not being able to explain why the polynomial model would be better in the chosen scenario.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Correctly states that Linear Regression models a straight-line relationship (1 mark). • Correctly states that Polynomial Regression models a curved relationship (1 mark). • Describes a suitable scenario where the relationship between variables is non-linear (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer:
The primary distinction between Linear and Polynomial Regression is the type of relationship they can model. Linear Regression assumes a linear relationship between the independent variable(s) and the dependent variable, meaning it always fits a straight line to the data.

Polynomial Regression extends the linear model by adding polynomial terms (e.g., x², x³) of the independent variables as new features. This allows it to model non-linear, or curved, relationships. Although it fits a curve to the data, it is still considered a linear model because the equation is linear in terms of its coefficients.

A scenario where Polynomial Regression would be more suitable is modelling the effect of advertising spend on revenue. Initially, as ad spend increases, revenue might increase sharply. However, after a certain point of market saturation, increasing ad spend further may yield diminishing returns, causing the revenue curve to flatten out. This creates a curved relationship that a simple straight line (Linear Regression) cannot capture accurately. A Polynomial Regression model could fit a curve to this data, providing a much more accurate prediction of revenue at different levels of ad spend.

Linear regression draws a straight line through the data. Polynomial regression can draw a curved line. You would use polynomial regression when the data doesn't follow a straight line. For example, the relationship between temperature and crop growth might be a curve, because crops grow well when it's warm but die if it gets too hot.
Linear regression uses lines and polynomial regression uses polynomials.

prompts.question: Design a Python class hierarchy using object-oriented principles to implement both linear and polynomial regression models that share common functionality (fit, predict, evaluate).
prompts.scenario: A teaching team wants a reusable, extensible regression library for classroom demonstrations.
prompts.markingCriteria: • Base/abstract class with shared methods (3 marks) • Correct subclass implementation (3 marks) • Use of sklearn pipeline or manual maths (2 marks).

sampleAnswers.answer: Base class RegressionModel with abstract methods fit() and predict(), plus shared evaluate() using MSE/R2. Subclasses LinearRegression (uses sklearn LinearRegression or manual gradient descent) and PolynomialRegression (uses PolynomialFeatures + LinearRegression pipeline). This promotes code reuse and polymorphism.

prompts.question: Implement a LogisticRegression class in Python that inherits from a base classifier and uses scikit-learn under the hood while exposing predict_proba().
